Added two new product-level items, each corroborated across at least two independent sources: `Hand-thrown pottery (Thamaga & Gabane)` -- sokarilondon.co.uk's "The Bowl That Refused to Be Ordinary" names Thamaga, Gabane, Molepolole and Gaborone as the country's pottery centres, using kaolin-based clay, oxide-painted geometric/animal motifs, describing contemporary work as "old knowledge in new hands"; independently corroborated by a Nircle feature specifically on Thamaga Pottery (founded 1985, staffed by local women "trained from scratch," now "a nationally recognized center for handmade ceramic art" with an on-site shop and studio tours, confirmed still operating today) and by botswana.co.za's country guide, which separately names Thamaga, Molepolole, Kanye and Gaborone as active commercial pottery centres today. `Hand-tanned leather goods (San & Tswana leatherwork)` -- botswanacraft.com's "San Crafts" page describes San artisans making carrying bags, dancing skirts and loin aprons for sale today, stating "increased production and sale of these items also provides them with a crucial source of cash income"; independently corroborated by Brighton & Hove Museums' "Making Botswana: Leatherwork" (leatherworking "has continued in Botswana," names a contemporary leathersmith, Edwin Keipedile, and references an annual leatherwork exhibition at the National Museum of Botswana) and by botswana.co.za confirming Bushman-made leather goods are sold at cooperative outlets found throughout the country. Checked both for this file's own restricted-material and no-weapons rules: clay and tanned hide only, no ivory/coral/bone.

**Deliberately not added, two candidates researched and rejected:** (1) San ostrich-eggshell bead jewellery -- genuinely well-corroborated as a real, currently-made-and-sold craft across three independent sources (botswanacraft.com's hand-drilling process description; botswana.co.za noting Bushman ostrich-shell crafting sold at cooperative outlets; womensworkbw.com, a dedicated seller of the product) -- but excluded because it is literally a shell product, and this file's own header rule bars "restricted-material products (ivory, coral, shell)" as hints. Ostrich eggshell is not an endangered-species or CITES-restricted material the way tortoiseshell or certain corals are, but this run chose not to make that distinction unilaterally: `lib/cultureHints.ts` already has a direct, on-the-record precedent for exactly this judgment call (the Maldives entry excluded coconut-shell items "even though the shell here is botanical (coconut), not animal, to stay unambiguous with the rule as written"), so treating animal eggshell as an exception while botanical shell was not one would be an inconsistent, unilateral reinterpretation of a rule this agent does not have standing to loosen. Flagging here, not deciding: if William wants ostrich-eggshell jewellery included (it is a strong, genuinely well-documented candidate), the header comment's shell rule would need to be explicitly narrowed first, e.g. to "animal-derived shell from CITES-restricted species," rather than this agent quietly carving out an exception. (2) San hunting equipment (bows, spears, "love bows") and quiver bags -- weapons and weapon-adjacent items, excluded per this file's standing no-weapons rule, the same call already applied to Kazakhstan's kamcha riding whip and Benin's récade ceremonial sceptre.

Camara, and its documented 2008-2013 collaboration with Tory Burch and the There Is No Limit Foundation, which engaged over 300 women tie-dyers and brought their work to retailers including Bloomingdale's and Galeries Lafayette Paris -- a real, still-referenced, currently-active tradition, not a defunct one. ### 2026-07-15 11:xx UTC — Benin `cultureHints.ts` expanded 1 -> 3 items, continuing backlog item 25's content-depth floor list

**Found:** Full audit not due (last full audit 2026-07-14 19:xx UTC, ~16h ago, well under the 24h floor) -- worked the backlog instead. Backlog item 25 (content-depth) is the standing top priority; Benin (`BJ`) was next on the floor-of-1 priority list after Maldives.

**Did:** Researched Benin/Fon/Dahomey crafts via WebSearch/WebFetch (not invented). Kept the existing `Abomey appliqué tapestries` entry untouched (not re-verified or renamed this run). Added two new, multi-source-verified items: `Bocio wood-carved Vodun figures (Ouidah)` -- Wikipedia's "Vodun art" article describes bocio as the primary wood-carved Vodun art form, names still-working Beninese artists (Cyprien Tokoudagba, Calixte Dakpogan, Theodore Dakpogan and others) and the 1993 International Festival of Vodun Arts and Cultures in Ouidah, independently corroborated by a travel guide (takeyourbackpack.com) describing hand-carved Vodun wood sculptures and masks currently sold at Cotonou's Artisanal Center; and `Hand-dyed indigo cotton textiles (Parakou)` -- an Oko Farms profile of Isidore Kouton, founder of CEPROMET (Centre de Promotion des Métiers du textile) in Parakou, northern Benin, describing organic indigo dyeing and hand-weaving as "still actively practiced and taught," having trained roughly 1,500 adults and nearly 2,000 students over ten years.

**Deliberately not added, two candidates researched and rejected:** (1) Récade -- Fon royal ceremonial sceptres. Multiple sources (Abomey's own tourism-office director, quoted via 100pour100culture.com, confirming local artisans still make them today; a Le Havre museum collection page; an MIT Press African Arts journal article) confirm this is a real, still-made craft tradition -- but the Le Havre museum's own description of an example with a "metal blade... employed as a weapon for executioners," and a militaria dealer (Oriental Arms) listing it under "Recade, Ceremonial Axe of the Fon, Benin," make this too weapon-adjacent for this file's standing no-weapons rule, the same judgment call already applied to Kazakhstan's kamcha riding whip. (2) Asen -- Fon iron memorial-altar staffs, extremely well documented as a historical art form by the Met Museum (three separate catalogued pieces), the Art Institute of Chicago, the Smithsonian National Museum of African Art, Cornell's museum, and a dedicated Barbier-Mueller Museum exhibition essay -- but every one of those sources describes 19th- to early-20th-century production specifically, and none confirms asen are still forged today. One Wikipedia page that might have settled this, "Benin ancestral altars," turned out on inspection to be entirely about the unrelated Edo people and Kingdom of Benin in Nigeria, not the Fon people or the Republic of Benin -- the same Benin-Bronzes/Nigeria-vs-Benin-country naming confusion this run was careful to avoid throughout (an initial web search for "Benin bronze casting" returned exclusively Nigerian Edo/Benin City results and was discarded in full rather than risk misattributing Nigerian heritage to this country). Per this log's own honesty standard, asen was left out rather than assumed to still be a living craft.

**Verified:** `npx --yes esbuild lib/cultureHints.ts --bundle=false --outfile=/dev/null` ran clean, zero errors. A Node script confirmed the total country-key count is unchanged at 191 and `BJ` now reads back with exactly 3 entries. Diffed the fetched `SEO_LOG.md`/`lib/cultureHints.ts` against the freshly-cloned repo tip before editing to confirm no concurrent changes were clobbered.

(HTTP 403) seen in prior runs -- confirmed not a token problem (never a 401). `raw.githubusercontent.com` worked fine for the initial read (HTTP 200). Also caught and resolved a real source discrepancy on the reed-mat material: three independent sources (expo2020maldives.gov.mv, encyclocraftsapr.com, maldives-magazine.com) describe the material as dried/dyed reed or screw-pine leaf, while a fourth (thearrival.mv) described it as coconut palm leaf -- went with the three agreeing sources, including the official government one, and logged the discrepancy in the file's own inline comment rather than silently picking one. `handicrafts.mv`, the Maldives National Chamber's own handicraft registry, returned a 500 server error on both pages attempted (mat weaving, coir rope) and could not be used as a source this run -- noted rather than treated as corroboration that wasn't actually obtained. No weapons found in the Maldives source material this run.

`lib/cultureHints.ts`'s `MV` entry: `['Lacquer boxes', 'Woven reed mats (Thundu Kunaa)', 'Coir rope (Roanu Veshun)', 'Miniature dhoni boat models']` -- kept the pre-existing `Lacquer boxes` entry unchanged (not re-verified or renamed this run), added three new product-level items, each corroborated across at least two independent Maldives-specific sources. **Research, multi-source verified, nothing invented:** `WebSearch` for Qatar traditional handicrafts, UNESCO intangible cultural heritage, bisht-making, naqdah embroidery, and Souq Waqif pottery/jewellery, followed by targeted `WebFetch` against primary and institutional/press sources. `visitqatar.com` (Qatar's official tourism board)'s own arts-and-crafts page confirmed Al-Sadu weaving, an-naqdah gold/silver-thread embroidery, gypsum carving, goldsmithing, henna, and pottery as the country's recognised traditional craft categories; cross-referenced against `ich.unesco.org`'s Qatar state page (fetched via WebFetch), which confirmed Qatar's own 2025 UNESCO inscriptions for "Traditional weaving of Al Sadu" and "Bisht (men's Abaa): skills and practices" specifically (not just a shared-Gulf inscription). Bisht-making was independently corroborated as a currently active, currently sold craft by The Peninsula Qatar's dedicated Souq Waqif feature (named craftsman, real price range QR1,800-9,000) and Qatar Tribune's Katara "Bisht House" coverage -- two independent Qatari press sources, not just the tourism board. Al-Sadu weaving was independently corroborated by Qatar Museums' own Al Sadu page, the Qatar Ministry of Culture's inventory entry, Marhaba Qatar's dedicated piece, and Wikipedia's Al Sadu article. Naqdah embroidery was independently corroborated by ILoveQatar.net's traditional-arts guide, which confirmed it remains in active use today (including by contemporary Qatari fashion brands), not just a historical technique. Pottery was independently corroborated by Gulf Times' Souq Waqif potter feature as a real, currently-operating, currently-selling craft -- flagged honestly that the specific profiled workshop is run by an Egyptian-born potter working in Qatar, so the fact verified is "pottery is a real, sold Qatari-souq craft category," not "this specific artisan is ethnically Qatari." Gypsum-carved decor rests on the single visitqatar.com source only (no independent second source found this run) -- included as a genuine official-source-documented craft category but flagged here, honestly, as the one item of the five with single-source backing rather than the two-plus-source bar the other four cleared.

**Research, multi-source verified, nothing invented:** `WebSearch` for Turkmenistan traditional crafts/handicrafts/jewelry/embroidery, followed by targeted `WebFetch` against primary and institutional sources. UNESCO's own Intangible Cultural Heritage site (`ich.unesco.org`) confirmed two separate, independently-inscribed Turkmen crafts: "Traditional Turkmen carpet making art in Turkmenistan" (corroborating the pre-existing `Hand-knotted Turkmen carpets` entry, kept unchanged) and "Turkmen-style needlework art" (fetched directly -- silk-thread loop-stitch embroidery on garments/accessories, passed down informally from mother to daughter). Kalpak Travel's and central-asia.guide's Turkmenistan souvenir guides (both fetched directly) independently corroborated carpets, silver-and-carnelian jewellery, embroidered textiles (keteni), and telpek sheepskin hats as the country's real, currently-sold craft categories. `Keteni silk fabric` was verified as a distinct product from the embroidery applied to it via its own dedicated page on the Encyclopedia of Crafts in the WCC-Asia Pacific Region (fetched directly -- homespun mulberry silk, hand-loomed on narrow `tara` looms, naturally dyed with madder/indigo/pomegranate skin/walnut), further corroborated by Turkmenistan's own government heritage-press coverage (turkmenistan.gov.tm, orient.tm, both surfaced by `WebSearch`) describing it as a still-practised craft, not a historical curiosity. `Telpek sheepskin hats` were independently confirmed via a dedicated Wikipedia article and live Etsy listings (both surfaced by `WebSearch`), establishing it as an actively-made and actively-sold product, not just a museum piece. `Silver jewellery with carnelian & turquoise` was corroborated by the Metropolitan Museum of Art's own "Turkmen Jewelry" essay and collection (surfaced by `WebSearch`, description cross-checked against Kalpak Travel and central-asia.guide's own jewellery entries). `Camel wool textiles` required a second, independent search specifically to avoid relying on a single source: the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage's "Camel Craft" feature (fetched directly -- a real Turkmen mother-daughter weaving enterprise, over fifteen women artisans, products including bags/scarves/pillows/runners from hand-loomed camel hair) was corroborated by a second, independent Smithsonian source, the National Museum of Asian Art's own "Turkmen textiles" collection notes, confirmed present in the same `WebSearch` results rather than assumed.

Five product-level additions plus the existing carpets entry kept unchanged: `Turkmen-style needlework embroidery`, `Keteni silk fabric`, `Silver jewellery with carnelian & turquoise`, `Telpek sheepskin hats`, and `Camel wool textiles` are new; `Hand-knotted Turkmen carpets` is untouched. **Deliberately not added:** Akhal-Teke horse memorabilia (statues, paintings, embroidered patches) -- appeared in central-asia.guide's souvenir guide but read as generic tourist merchandise rather than a distinct traditional craft category, the same bar already applied to excluding generic items in prior countries' expansions; ceramics/wooden boxes/metalwork (Kalpak Travel only, and central-asia.guide explicitly noted it does *not* mention ceramics or musical instruments as notable Turkmenistan souvenirs -- a single-source item was held back rather than added, consistent with this file's multi-source standard).

**Edit made: `lib/cultureHints.ts` only.** `TM` array extended from 1 to 6 items, with a full inline citation comment above the entry (same convention as every prior country-expansion entry in this file). `app/sitemap.ts` and `app/origins/layout.tsx` both already filter on `cultureHints(code).length > 0`, so `/origins/turkmenistan`'s existing sitemap entry and `ItemList` row need no separate edit -- Turkmenistan already had 1 item, above the `> 0` threshold, and simply now carries richer on-page content. Verified with `npx --yes esbuild lib/cultureHints.ts --bundle=false --outfile=/dev/null`, zero errors (syntax-only check; full `prisma generate`/type-check is not possible from this sandbox, same limitation prior sessions have hit against the Prisma binaries CDN). Also verified with a small Node script that extracts and evaluates just the `CULTURE_HINTS` object literal (stopping at its matching closing brace, before the file's later `export function cultureHints(...)` block, which has TypeScript type annotations `eval` can't parse): `TM`'s new array reads back correctly as six strings and the total country-key count is unchanged at 191.

**Backlog:** item 25 updated -- `TM` removed from the floor-of-1 list (16 remaining: `QA, BH, HK, BN, MV, BJ, GM, GN, SL, BW, NA, GA, SD, LI, LU, NO`), Turkmenistan's contribution logged. No other item touched.

**Push: confirmed pushed, verified two independent ways.** `git clone https://github.com/BILSY144/velor-marketplace.git` worked directly against `github.com` in this sandbox (no local-proxy remote and no PAT needed for the read); latest commit on `main` at clone time was `087ceb5` ("Schema + Open Graph logo swapped to the 2026 brand mark"), two commits ahead of this log's own last-recorded push (`25ed14d`/`986564c`) -- both unrelated brand-logo commits from a separate session, not something this run needed to reconcile content-wise. Copied the locally-edited, syntax-verified `lib/cultureHints.ts` into the clone and wrote this log update, then set `origin` to `https://x-access-token:<PAT>@github.com/BILSY144/velor-marketplace.git` (PAT never echoed to any log, commit message, or committed file) and committed both files together. `git push origin main` succeeded on the first attempt, no proxy 403 and no rejected-fetch-first conflict this cycle. Verified the push landed two independent ways: a fully anonymous `git ls-remote https://github.com/BILSY144/velor-marketplace.git main` (no credentials at all) returned a SHA matching local `HEAD` exactly, and `git fetch origin main` immediately after, followed by `git rev-list --count` in both directions, returned `0`/`0`. Afterward, reset `origin` back to the plain, credential-free `https://github.com/BILSY144/velor-marketplace.git` URL and confirmed via `git remote -v` and a `.git/config` grep that zero occurrences of the PAT string remain anywhere in the local clone.

Second, per the task brief's explicit callout of content depth on `/origins/[country]` pages against the "15+ researched items" standing rule -- a target this log's backlog had only ever described qualitatively, never measured -- this run wrote a small Node script to count every `CULTURE_HINTS` entry's array length directly from `lib/cultureHints.ts` rather than estimating. Result: 191 entries (190 real `WORLD_COUNTRIES` plus the already-known orphaned `CI`, item 23), average 3.0 items each, with 20+ countries sitting at a floor of exactly 1 item. This is a real, honest gap against the standing target, and a large one -- logged in full as new backlog item 25 rather than left as a vague "multi-session undertaking" note.

**Found and fixed, one country this run:** Indonesia (`ID`) had only 3 items (`Batik sarongs`, `Rattan homeware`, `Teak carvings`) despite being one of the world's most craft-diverse countries -- a natural first pick both for its own sake and as a template for the item-25 pattern going forward. `WebSearch` (not `WebFetch`, same reasoning as the 2026-07-15 00:xx UTC run: can return `PROVENANCE_REQUIRED` in unattended sessions with no one present to approve) for Indonesian traditional crafts, cross-checked against UNESCO's own Intangible Cultural Heritage listings where applicable. Five product-level additions, each multi-source verified, none single-sourced or from a suspected content-farm domain: **Songket weaving** (gold/silver-thread ceremonial weaving historically centred on Palembang, Sumatra) -- Wikipedia, Google Arts & Culture's "Woven Tales of Indonesia: Songket Palembang" feature, and the National Gallery of Australia's Indonesian Textiles collection notes. **Ikat weaving** (tenun ikat, a distinct Indonesian resist-dye tradition, not the same product already listed as `UZ`'s "Ikat silks") -- the same National Gallery of Australia notes plus Natural Dye Store's dedicated piece on Indonesian weaving styles. **Silver filigree jewellery** from Kotagede, Yogyakarta -- has its own Wikipedia article (Kotagede) plus independent tourism/craft coverage (Java Heritage Tour, Jogjalanjalan, YogyakartaTour.com, an ANTARA Foto photo-story) describing it as a still-active, centuries-old craft rather than a historical curiosity. **Wayang kulit** shadow puppets -- UNESCO-inscribed Intangible Cultural Heritage ("Wayang puppet theatre," ich.unesco.org) with its own Wikipedia article. **Angklung** bamboo instruments -- separately UNESCO-inscribed, documented on UNESCO's own Silk Roads Programme site. No shell/ivory/coral/bone material in any of the five; wayang kulit puppets are traditionally buffalo/goat leather, the same accepted leather-goods category this file already uses elsewhere (e.g. `IN`'s `'Leather juttis'`). No protected geographical indication used loosely.

**Edit made: `lib/cultureHints.ts` only.** `ID` array extended from 3 to 8 items, with a full inline citation comment above the entry (same convention as the 2026-07-14/15 Andorra/Monaco entries). `app/sitemap.ts` and `app/origins/layout.tsx` both already filter on `cultureHints(code).length > 0`, so `/origins/indonesia`'s sitemap entry and `ItemList` row need no separate edit -- they already existed (Indonesia already had 3 items, well above the `> 0` threshold) and simply now carry richer on-page content. This sandbox's pre-wired proxy remote (read-only for push, per the 2026-07-12 rules-section note) returned the expected plain `403` on `git push`; swapped to `git remote set-url origin "https://x-access-token:<PAT>@github.com/BILSY144/velor-marketplace.git"` and pushed directly to `github.com` over HTTPS, which succeeded. ### 2026-07-14 22:xx UTC — Site-wide `robots.googleBot` directive added, opting every indexable page into large-image search previews -- a genuinely new gap no prior run had checked

Full audit was not due (last full audit still 2026-07-14 19:12 UTC, `609d493`, ~3h old at this run's start, ~22:07 UTC -- checked this log's own timestamps before doing anything else, not assumed). Re-checked every numbered backlog item (1-24) before looking further: all are still either resolved, blocked on real catalogue/seller data not existing yet, or an explicit judgment call already flagged for William -- same state the 20:xx/21:xx UTC runs found. So, like those two runs, this one did a fresh diligence pass for a new safe/additive gap rather than re-flagging the same judgment calls.

`api.github.com`'s Contents API returned `403 {"message":"GitHub access to this repository is not enabled for this session. Use add_repo to request access."}` on every attempt (`https_proxy`/`HTTPS_PROXY` were not set in this shell to begin with, so the 21:xx UTC run's "unset the proxy vars first" workaround didn't apply here -- noted for a future run to actually test that variable-unset step deliberately, since this run had nothing to unset). Fell back to `raw.githubusercontent.com` (worked, unauthenticated) to read this log and `CLAUDE.md` first. `git clone https://github.com/BILSY144/velor-marketplace.git` worked immediately via this sandbox's pre-wired local-proxy remote, same as every prior run. Latest commit at clone time was `7b9c264` (the 21:xx UTC run's own logo fix), confirming no other session had touched the repo in between.

**Found and fixed, this run -- not a pre-existing backlog item:** a repo-wide grep for `googleBot`, `max-image-preview`, `max-snippet`, and `max-video-preview` returned zero matches anywhere in the codebase -- confirmed no prior run had ever checked this specific directive family, distinct from the `robots: {index, follow}` indexability checks many prior runs (and this log's own rules section) already cover thoroughly. Verified directly against Google's own current documentation (`WebFetch` against `developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/robots-meta-tag`, quoted verbatim this run): "If you don't specify the `max-image-preview` rule, Google may show an image preview of the default size" -- i.e. large image thumbnails in Search results are opt-in, not automatic, and a site that never sets this directive is left on Google's smaller default preview size. Velor is an inherently visual, product-photography-led marketplace per `CLAUDE.md`'s own positioning, so this is a real, if easy-to-miss, cost once the catalogue and the already-live `/origins/[slug]` country pages have real images for Google to show at full size.

Also checked, before adding this site-wide, whether it could weaken any existing `noindex` page: a repo-wide grep for `robots:` found exactly 7 files that set their own `metadata.robots` field -- `/search`, `/apply/invited`, `/unsubscribe`, `not-found.tsx`, `/origins/[slug]`'s invalid-slug branch, `/pulse`, and `/live/[room]` -- all already deliberately `noindex` (verified by reading each, not assumed; same 7 this log's rules/backlog already document as intentional). Next.js metadata fields are replaced wholesale per key rather than deep-merged (the same behaviour this codebase's own `app/layout.tsx` comments already document for `openGraph`, citing `vercel/next.js#50353`), so a child route that defines its own `robots` object is entirely unaffected by whatever the parent sets -- confirmed this is genuinely additive and cannot flip any of those 7 pages indexable or change their behaviour in any way.

**Edit made: `app/layout.tsx` only.** Added `robots: { index: true, follow: true, googleBot: { index: true, follow: true, 'max-image-preview': 'large', 'max-snippet': -1, 'max-video-preview': -1 } }` to the root metadata export, with an inline comment citing the Google doc and explaining the inheritance reasoning above. `index: true`/`follow: true` at both levels are explicit restatements of Next.js's own implicit default (this file had no `robots` field at all before), so this changes nothing about indexability -- only the preview size and snippet/video-preview limits for pages already indexed. `max-snippet: -1` and `max-video-preview: -1` are Google's own documented "no limit" values, included alongside `max-image-preview: 'large'` since all three live in the same directive family and this is the one shared place to set them site-wide. No title, description, canonical, openGraph, twitter, viewport, or JSON-LD field touched. Verified with `npx --yes esbuild "app/layout.tsx" --bundle=false --outfile=/dev/null`, zero errors. Also ran a full `npx tsc --noEmit -p tsconfig.json` this run (not attempted by several recent runs) -- it still cannot complete cleanly in this sandbox (`node_modules` isn't installed; every error is a "Cannot find module 'next'/'react'/etc." or a pre-existing implicit-`any` warning elsewhere in the codebase, e.g. `lib/orders.ts`, `lib/dashboard-theme.tsx`), but critically **zero errors reference the new `robots` field or the `Metadata` type itself** -- the only `app/layout.tsx` errors are the same missing-module-declaration class affecting every file in the project, not something this change introduced. `git diff --stat` showed exactly the intended scope: 1 file changed, 39 insertions, 0 deletions.

**Backlog:** unchanged -- this was a same-run find-and-fix, not a pre-existing numbered item, and not a new open judgment call worth adding a number for (no design or content decision required, unlike the favicon gap).

**Push: confirmed pushed, verified not assumed -- and a correction to what this entry originally claimed.** `git fetch origin main` immediately before committing confirmed zero drift both directions against the pre-wired local-proxy remote (`HEAD` matched `origin/main`, no new commits since the 21:xx UTC run's push). Committed `app/layout.tsx` and this `SEO_LOG.md` update together. `git push origin main` through the local-proxy remote returned a plain `403` with no body, same as every recent run. Applied the documented workaround: `git remote set-url origin` to `https://x-access-token:<PAT>@github.com/BILSY144/velor-marketplace.git`, pushed directly to github.com over HTTPS with the session's PAT -- succeeded (`7b9c264..cb5b479 main -> main`). Verified with a separate, fully anonymous `git ls-remote https://github.com/BILSY144/velor-marketplace.git main`, which returned `cb5b479382c70b05d952afbf08befaea7070e8c3` -- matching `git rev-parse HEAD` on the local clone exactly. That is sufficient on its own to confirm the push landed, independent of the proxy. **A first draft of this paragraph, written before this second check, claimed the post-push `git fetch origin main` against the reset local-proxy remote "also succeeded this time" -- that was wrong, caught and corrected before this entry was finalized rather than left in:** that fetch attempt actually failed with the exact same `fatal: could not read Username for 'http://127.0.0.1:41729': terminal prompts disabled` error the 19:xx UTC run hit, not a success. The `git rev-list --count` 0/0 result that followed it was misleading, not a genuine confirmation -- `git push` updates its own remote-tracking ref locally as a side effect of a successful push regardless of which URL was used, so `origin/main` was already showing `cb5b479` from the push itself, before the fetch attempt failed; the failed fetch never actually reached the remote to re-verify anything. Verification for this run rests entirely on the one check that actually succeeded and doesn't depend on the proxy at all: the anonymous `ls-remote` match above. The remote was reset back to the plain local-proxy URL immediately after the push, confirmed via `git remote -v` and a `.git/config` grep that no PAT was left embedded anywhere. The PAT was used only in the remote URL for the push command itself and was never echoed to any log, commit message, or file.

**Vercel deployment:** not independently checked this run, per this log's own standing rule -- Vercel auto-notifies William directly, by web and email, on any deployment failure. This is a low-risk, purely additive, metadata-only change (one new JSON object in `<head>`-level page metadata; no logic touched, no visual change to any rendered page element).

**Found and fixed, this run -- not a pre-existing backlog item:** re-read the full `Organization` JSON-LD in `app/layout.tsx` against schema.org/Google's own logo requirements directly (`WebFetch` against `developers.google.com/search/docs/appearance/structured-data/organization`, quoted verbatim: "The image must be 112x112px, at minimum"), something no prior run's own "structured-data saturation check" (see the 2026-07-13 log entries referencing that phrase) had actually verified -- every prior pass checked which *properties* existed (`logo`, `sameAs`, `address`, etc.), never the logo image's actual pixel dimensions against Google's stated minimum. Checked directly with PIL, not assumed: `public/velor-logo-globe-v2.png`, the file the `Organization.logo` property has pointed at since it was first added (2026-07-13), is 206x60px -- its 60px height sits well under Google's 112px floor, so the logo was very likely ineligible for Google's logo rich result / knowledge-panel logo despite the rest of the Organization node being fully populated (address, sameAs, email, contactPoint, etc., all added by earlier runs).

Fix: swapped `Organization.logo` to `public/velor-logo.png`, 608x118px (also confirmed via PIL -- both dimensions clear the 112px minimum, no aspect-ratio requirement exists in Google's docs so the non-square shape is not a concern). This is not a new or unused asset picked at random: `app/opengraph-image.tsx` already uses this exact file as the real, live brand mark rendered into every page's shipped Open Graph image, so this is a like-for-like swap to an already-approved, already-in-production asset -- not a new design decision, unlike the still-open favicon gap (backlog item 12), which does require a genuinely new square asset nobody has supplied yet. The header/footer's own `<img>` logo (`components/GlobalHeader.tsx`, `components/GlobalFooter.tsx`) still correctly uses `velor-logo-globe-v2.png` at 28-34px UI height -- appropriately sized for that use and deliberately untouched, since this fix is scoped only to the JSON-LD structured-data property, not the visible site logo.

**Edit made: `app/layout.tsx` only** (one JSON-LD string value plus an explanatory comment; `title`, `description`, `areaServed`, `address`, `sameAs`, `email`, `contactPoint`, the `WebSite` node, canonical/robots, and every other field left untouched). ### 2026-07-14 20:xx UTC — Worked backlog item 22: found a genuinely new, multi-source-verifiable cultureHints entry for Andorra; Monaco re-searched, still unresolved

Re-checked every numbered backlog item before picking one: items 1, 2, 3, 8, 9, 11, 12, 16, 20, 21, 23, 24 are all still either resolved, blocked on real catalogue/seller data not existing yet, or explicit judgment calls already flagged for William -- none are this agent's to unilaterally decide, per the standing additive-by-default rule. Item 22 (cultureHints coverage) was the one substantive, genuinely-researchable item left open, explicitly inviting a fresh attempt "if a future run finds a genuinely new, product-level, multi-source-verifiable angle." This run tried a different tactic than prior attempts: searching in Andorra's and Monaco's own languages (Catalan/Spanish for Andorra, French for Monaco) instead of English, on the theory that English-language search results for two small European microstates skew toward generic tourist-souvenir content regardless of how many times re-tried.

**Andorra — added.** A Catalan/Spanish-language search surfaced Andorra's official government tourism site's local-products page (visitandorra.com/en/gastronomy/local-products/), which names real, specific producers rather than generic categories. Cross-referencing two of those producers against independent sources found strong multi-source corroboration for two: **FORA Gin** — a real, working distillery in Ordino parish, corroborated by Andorra's own Ordino parish tourism site (visitordino.com), Andorran news outlet alto.ad (covering a biosphere-reserve certification for the distillery), and independently by UK packaging trade press (Label and Narrow Web, Packaging News, both covering "FORA gin captures the spirit of Andorra") -- three independent sources, not one. **Xocland** — an Andorran chocolate maker listed directly on the official tourism site's shopping section (visitandorra.com/es/compras/xocland) and independently covered by four separate Andorran travel/lifestyle sites (esquiades.com, principado-de-andorra.com, hoyandorra.com, hoteldeltarter.com) describing an established "Ruta del Chocolate" (chocolate route) built around it as a real, ongoing local attraction, not a one-off mention. Added `AD: ['Craft gin', 'Artisan chocolate']` to `lib/cultureHints.ts`, named generically per this file's existing convention (product category, not brand name -- matching e.g. `CH: ['Watches', 'Chocolate']`, not a specific Swiss chocolatier). **Deliberately did not add three other products found on the same official tourism page:** Vital's shepherd's wool cushions and Casa Raubert's sheep's-milk cheese are both single-sourced (only that one page mentions them, no independent corroboration found); Ratafia liqueur (found via a separate search) turned out to be a broader Catalan-region product with its own protected geographical indication (IGP Ratafia catalana, confirmed via gastroteca.cat and multiple Catalan food-heritage sites) rather than something distinctly Andorran, so labelling it as an Andorran product risked this file's own standing "no protected geographical indications used loosely" rule -- flagged and excluded rather than stretched to fill a quota. This brings the real per-country cultureHints split (excluding the orphaned `CI` key, backlog item 23, still unresolved) from 188/190 to 189/190 (99.5%).

**Monaco — re-searched, still no genuinely new lead.** A French-language search ("artisanat monégasque traditionnel") surfaced mostly modern trades (joinery, upholstery, painters/sculptors) rather than culturally distinctive traditional products, plus the same generic souvenir-listicle and luxury-brand-shop results prior runs already found. One promising-looking lead -- Fragonard perfumery, which repeatedly surfaces in "what to buy in Monaco" searches -- was checked directly: multiple independent sources (fragonard.com, seemonaco.com, evendo.com) confirm the actual factory and visitor experience are in Eze-Village and Grasse, France, not Monaco itself, so it was correctly not added as a Monaco product despite showing up in Monaco-adjacent search results. No new Monaco entry added this run; same "do not re-add on the same evidence already rejected" standard applied.

**Edit made:** `lib/cultureHints.ts` only (one new object entry plus a citation comment, in the microstate cluster near San Marino/Vatican City). No other file touched -- `app/sitemap.ts` and `app/origins/layout.tsx` both auto-pick-up the new Andorra entry via their existing `cultureHints(code).length > 0` filter, confirmed by reading both files' filter logic directly rather than assumed.

Applied the documented workaround: `git remote set-url origin` to `https://x-access-token:<PAT>@github.com/BILSY144/velor-marketplace.git`, pushed directly to github.com over HTTPS with the session's PAT -- succeeded. Verified two independent ways: `git fetch origin main` + `git rev-list --count` both directions against the PAT-authenticated remote returned `0`/`0` (local `HEAD` and `origin/main` identical), and a separate, fully anonymous `git ls-remote https://github.com/BILSY144/velor-marketplace.git main` independently returned the same commit hash as local `HEAD`. **Full checklist pass, re-verifying rather than trusting old conclusions:**
- **sitemap.xml / robots.txt:** both re-read from source, still correct -- `/admin/` prefix rule confirmed to cover all 7 new `app/admin/*` subdirectories; the 4 previously-added private-route rules (`/account`, `/messages`, `/activate`, `/setup-admin`) still present and still the only gaps that existed.

- **Image alt text:** a repo-wide `<img` grep (`app` + `components`) flagged 7 hits without `alt=` on the same source line; read all 7 in full context (`app/seller/[sellerId]/page.tsx` x2, `app/marketplace/[id]/ProductDetail.tsx`, `app/orders/page.tsx`, `app/page.tsx`, `app/messages/page.tsx`, `components/CountryOriginStrip.tsx`) -- the same known multi-line-JSX grep false positive prior runs have documented; every one has a real, dynamic `alt` on the next line (`alt={product.title}`, `alt={seller.country}`, etc.), except `CountryOriginStrip.tsx`'s decorative country-flag icon, which deliberately uses `alt=""` on a parent `<button>` that already carries `title={c.name}` and `aria-label={`Shop products from ${c.name}`}` -- verified by reading the surrounding JSX, not assumed. Zero real gaps.
- **Internal linking / broken links:** extracted every `href="/..."` string across `app` and `components` and cross-checked against real routes under `app/`. All resolve to real, existing routes. Re-confirmed the one known issue is still present and still out of this agent's lane: `app/messages/page.tsx` redirects an unauthenticated visitor to `/auth/signin` (no hyphen), but the real route is `/auth/sign-in` -- a client-side auth-redirect bug, already flagged in backlog item 21, not an SEO/indexing defect since it 404s for a logged-out visitor rather than affecting any crawlable page.

**Found and fixed, this run -- not a pre-existing backlog item:** a codebase-wide scan of every `layout.tsx`/`page.tsx` exporting `metadata` (a Python script parsing both the `const description = '...'` and inline `description: '...'` patterns, since an earlier same-day scan, 2026-07-14 14:xx UTC, only checked the inline pattern and titles, and missed these because five of the six affected files use the `const description = ...` + shorthand-property pattern instead) surfaced two real, previously-unflagged gaps:

1. **Three real, live, canonical-tagged, sitemap-included pages had NO meta description at all:** `/track`, `/returns`, `/cookies` (`app/track/page.tsx`, `app/returns/page.tsx`, `app/cookies/page.tsx`) each had `title`, `alternates.canonical`, `openGraph`, and `twitter` fully set, but no `description` field anywhere in the file -- confirmed by grep, not eyeballed. This means search engines were auto-generating the SERP snippet for all three instead of Velor controlling it. Added a `description` const to each, drawn directly from the page's own live body copy (verified by reading each file's JSX, nothing invented) -- `/track`: 114 chars ("Sign in and open Your Orders to track a Velor order..."); `/returns`: 147 chars (each seller's own returns policy, contact via Your Orders); `/cookies`: 139 chars (essential + limited-analytics cookies, browser-level control). Wired the same const into each file's `metadata.description`, `openGraph.description`, and `twitter.description` (3 fields x 3 files).
2. **Six of the site's most important pages had meta descriptions well past Google's practical ~155-160 char SERP display limit** -- the same class of defect this log already fixed on the 4 `/legal/*` pages (2026-07-14 01:xx UTC), but this time on `/apply` (215 chars), `/shop` (203), `/origins` (202), `/founding` (195), `/sell` (180), and `/help` (169) -- exactly the seller-recruitment and key-navigation pages `CLAUDE.md` says should carry the most SEO weight right now, so getting these truncated mid-sentence in search results is a real cost, not a cosmetic one. Trimmed each to 133-156 chars: kept every concrete fact (24-hour decision, Pro free for life for founding sellers, 190 countries, the specific product categories on `/shop`, every FAQ category on `/help`, maker+origin on every listing) and cut only redundant phrasing that duplicated the title or another page's description -- no fact added, changed, or removed. Full before/after reasoning is in each file's own comment. `/origins`'s trimmed description is also reused verbatim in its `ItemList` JSON-LD `description` field (shared const), so that schema shortened too, automatically, with no separate edit.

**Edits made: 9 files** -- `app/track/page.tsx`, `app/returns/page.tsx`, `app/cookies/page.tsx` (new description added, 3 metadata fields each), `app/apply/layout.tsx`, `app/shop/layout.tsx`, `app/origins/layout.tsx`, `app/founding/layout.tsx`, `app/sell/layout.tsx`, `app/help/layout.tsx` (description trimmed in place, one `const` each, propagates to `metadata`/`openGraph`/`twitter` automatically since all six already used a shared-const pattern). Every currently-numbered backlog item was re-checked and is still either resolved, blocked on real catalogue/seller data not existing yet, or an explicit judgment call already flagged for William (items 1, 2, 3, 8, 9, 11, 12, 16, 20, 21, 23, 24) -- and item 22 (cultureHints coverage, Andorra/Monaco) is explicitly not to be re-attempted without genuinely new evidence, which this run did not go looking for again so soon after the 17:xx UTC attempt. **What this run checked, verified clean, and did not change:** (1) `app/robots.ts`'s disallow list against every real route under `app/` -- all private/session/token-gated routes found (`/account`, `/messages`, `/activate`, `/setup-admin`, `/dashboard/`, `/admin/`, `/checkout/`, `/orders`, `/api/`) are covered; `/pulse` is deliberately NOT in the disallow list, which is correct, not an oversight -- `app/pulse/layout.tsx` already carries its own `robots: { index: false, follow: false }` meta directive (verified by reading the file directly), and per standard SEO practice a page using meta-noindex should not also be blocked by robots.txt, since a robots.txt block prevents the crawler from ever reading the page far enough to see the noindex tag in the first place, which can paradoxically leave the URL indexed with no snippet if anything links to it. `/pulse`'s own pages additionally gate on a bearer-token check (`usePulseAuth`/`TokenGate` in `components/pulse/PulseKit.tsx`), not a `useSession` redirect like `/admin`, but that's an authentication detail, not an indexing one, and doesn't change the robots.txt conclusion. (2) A full internal-link inventory: extracted every `href="..."` and `href: '...'` string across `app/` and `components/` (two separate regex passes, since `GlobalFooter.tsx` builds its links from a `COLUMNS` data array using `href: '...'` object syntax, not JSX `href="..."` attributes -- the first pass alone under-counted and would have produced a false-positive orphan finding for `/returns`, which is in fact linked from the footer's "Your orders" column) and cross-checked the result against every real route under `app/`. (3) Confirmed `/blog`, `/careers`, `/press`, `/pricing` links found in this same scan come only from `components/Footer.tsx`, the already-flagged (backlog item 16) stray, unimported, dead file -- not live broken links, already logged, not re-flagged here.

**What this run found and fixed:** `/cookies` (`app/cookies/page.tsx`) -- a real, complete, live page with its own metadata, a self-referencing canonical (`https://velorcommerce.store/cookies`), and a sitemap.xml entry (per backlog item 3's canonical-batch list, both already shipped by prior runs) -- had **zero internal links pointing to it anywhere in the codebase**, confirmed by a repo-wide grep for both `'/cookies'` and `"/cookies"` returning no matches before this fix. It was reachable only by typing the URL directly or via sitemap.xml/search-engine crawl, the same orphan-page shape backlog item 20 already flagged for `/marketplace` -- except `/marketplace` was a deliberate judgment call (competing duplicate-content architecture question, not this agent's to decide), while this is a plain oversight: `/legal/privacy` and `/legal/terms` sit right next to each other in `GlobalFooter.tsx`'s "Company" column, and a cookie policy link is the standard third item alongside privacy/terms in that exact spot on essentially every commercial site, so its absence reads as a link a prior page redesign simply forgot to add, not a deliberate omission. Added `{ label: 'Cookies', href: '/cookies' }` as a sixth entry in that column, directly after `Terms` (`components/GlobalFooter.tsx`), reusing the exact same `Link`-mapping code the other five links in that column already use -- no new component, no styling change, no other column touched.

**Edit made:** `components/GlobalFooter.tsx` only (one new object literal in an existing typed array). Verified with `npx --yes esbuild "components/GlobalFooter.tsx" --bundle=false --outfile=/dev/null`, which compiled with zero errors (syntax-only check, not a full type-check -- this sandbox still cannot reach the Prisma binary CDN for a full `npx tsc --noEmit`, same limitation every recent run has hit).

**Backlog:** unchanged -- this was a same-run find-and-fix, not a pre-existing numbered item, and not a new open judgment call worth adding a number for.

**Push: confirmed pushed, verified not assumed.** Committed `components/GlobalFooter.tsx` and this `SEO_LOG.md` update together. `git fetch origin main` immediately before committing confirmed zero drift both directions against the pre-wired local-proxy remote. `git push origin main` through that proxy remote returned a plain `403` with no body, the same proxy-403-on-push failure mode every prior run since 2026-07-12 20:23 UTC has documented. Applied the documented workaround: `git remote set-url origin` to `https://x-access-token:<PAT>@github.com/BILSY144/velor-marketplace.git` and pushed directly to github.com over HTTPS with the session's PAT. Verified two ways, not just trusted from the push command's exit code: `git fetch origin main` + `git rev-list --count` both directions returned `0` after the push (local `HEAD` and `origin/main` identical), and a separate, fully anonymous `git ls-remote https://github.com/BILSY144/velor-marketplace.git main` independently confirmed the same commit hash as local `HEAD`. The remote was reset back to the plain local-proxy URL immediately afterward (PAT removed from `.git/config`, confirmed absent via grep). The PAT was used only in the remote URL for the push command itself and was not echoed to any log, commit message, or file.

**Vercel deployment:** not independently checked this run, per this log's own standing rule -- Vercel auto-notifies William directly, by web and email, on any deployment failure, and this sandbox has no general outbound network access to `api.vercel.com` regardless. This is a low-risk, purely additive change (one new link in an existing footer column, no altered component logic, no visual change to any other element).

Item 22 (cultureHints coverage) was the one item explicitly left open to revisit "if a future run finds a genuinely new, product-level, multi-source-verifiable angle" for one of its 4 remaining skipped countries -- so this run attempted exactly that, for all 4, rather than doing a generic fresh sweep:

- **Djibouti — added.** Prior runs (2026-07-14 10:xx UTC) found only a single non-authoritative lead. This run's `WebSearch` + `WebFetch` found two independent, higher-authority sources: blogs.worldbank.org's "Women in Djibouti make money weaving grass and pearls into baskets, belts" (a World Bank livelihoods-programme article, naming baskets and belts specifically as products rural women weave and sell) and harbingerstandard.com's "Handmade Crafts Anchor Djibouti's Cultural Heritage" (independently describing "tightly coiled palm-fiber" basket construction). Both corroborate woven grass/palm baskets as a real, sourced product. Added `DJ: ['Woven grass baskets', 'Woven palm-fiber belts']`. Deliberately did NOT add the embroidered "Gadha" shawl or hand-painted pottery the Harbinger Standard article also mentions -- those were single-sourced (that one article only), not carried forward per the same no-stretch-to-fill-a-quota discipline prior runs applied elsewhere. Confirmed neither product involves shell/ivory/coral/bone.
- **Antigua and Barbuda — added.** Prior run (2026-07-14 08:xx UTC) found only Betty's Hope, a historic plantation site, not a product. This run found and fetched two sources: visitantiguabarbuda.com (the official tourism board's own site, "Arts, Crafts & Cuisine: A Taste of Authentic Antiguan & Barbudan Life"), naming handwoven baskets/straw creations, pottery, and Barbuda sea-glass jewellery-making; and discoverantiguabarbuda.com's "Made in Antigua and Barbuda", independently corroborating handwoven baskets and separately naming "handwoven Sea Island cotton products" (a real historic Antiguan cotton tradition, not a brand) and guava cheese (also named on the tourism-board page as a traditional preserve). Added `AG: ['Handwoven straw baskets', 'Sea Island cotton textiles', 'Sea-glass jewellery', 'Guava cheese preserves']`. Deliberately excluded rum brand names (Cavalier, English Harbour) found on discoverantiguabarbuda.com -- brand names, not culture-first product hints, per this file's own header rule.
- **Andorra — re-researched, still skipped.** `WebSearch` results this run were dominated by `mexicohistorico.com` (an oddly-named domain for Andorran content, reading like generic AI-generated SEO filler rather than a real source -- not trusted) and unrelated wood-flooring product listings (a company literally named "Andorra" makes hickory flooring, a false-positive keyword match). - **The one genuine new gap found:** `app/not-found.tsx` (the custom 404 page, confirmed via `grep -rn "not-found\|NotFound"` across this entire log's history returning zero prior mentions -- genuinely never audited before) had no `metadata` export at all. Next.js already sends a real HTTP 404 status code for this file (App Router special-file convention, unrelated to the `metadata` export), but with no metadata of its own the page's `<title>`/`<meta description>` inherited the root layout's homepage copy ("Velor — Global Marketplace for Culture & Heritage" / the homepage's own description) on a page that is not the homepage, and carried no explicit `robots: noindex`. Google's own guidance recommends an explicit noindex signal on 404-type pages rather than relying solely on the HTTP status. Fixed additively: added `title: 'Page Not Found — Velor'`, a `description` that is a trimmed, near-verbatim reuse of this same file's own already-live visible body copy ("The product or page you're looking for may have been removed, sold out, or the link might be out of date. Try browsing the shop for similar items, or head back home.", shortened to 134 characters to fit Google's ~155-160 char display limit, the same length rule already applied to `/legal/*` -- see item 18), and `robots: { index: false, follow: true }`. Nothing invented; no other file touched.
- Verification: file has no `'use client'` directive, so the `metadata` export is valid in a server component context (confirmed by reading the file directly, not assumed). Could not run `npx tsc --noEmit` this run -- `node_modules` is not present in the fresh clone and a full `npm install` was not attempted (no prior run has recorded doing this either); the change follows the exact same `Metadata` type-import and object-shape pattern already used and type-checked in multiple sibling `layout.tsx` files in this codebase (e.g. the `robots: { index: false }` shape already used for `/unsubscribe`, `/apply/invited`), so it is a low-risk, precedented pattern, but noting the lack of a fresh `tsc` run honestly rather than claiming one happened.

No gap.

Three descriptions are short of a 70-char floor (`/apply/verified` 55, `/seller-agreement` 67, `/pulse` 33) -- all three already known/explained (first two: backlog-flagged low-priority pages; `/pulse` is the private noindex dashboard) -- and zero are over the ~160-char truncation ceiling. No new gap.
- **`/specialities` lattice:** the audit checklist's "internal linking across the /origins x /specialities lattice" language does not correspond to any real route in this codebase today -- `find app -iname "*specialit*"` returns nothing. Noting this as a scope clarification, not a bug: `/shop` covers speciality filtering via a query param (`?category=`), there is no separate `/specialities` section to link into. Not treating this as a gap to build against, consistent with the standing "don't build structure ahead of real data/decisions" rule.
- **Image alt text (the one real finding):** item 7's original "zero `<img>` missing `alt`" check used a single-line grep, which misses the common React pattern of `alt` on its own line -- re-checked with full multi-line context. Found 10 `<img>` tags codebase-wide with `alt=""` (as opposed to a missing `alt` attribute, which is still genuinely zero). Read every one in context before deciding: `components/CountryOriginStrip.tsx`'s flag icon is correctly empty -- its parent `<button>` already carries `aria-label={`Shop products from ${c.name}`}` and `title={c.name}`, so a second text alternative on the flag itself would be redundant, the correct WCAG pattern. `components/VelorAssistant.tsx`'s two chat-avatar `<img>`s are a repeated decorative icon in a message list -- also correctly empty, giving it real alt text would have a screen reader announce the same string on every message. `app/pulse/revenue|reviews|listings/page.tsx` (3 occurrences) and `app/dashboard/products/page.tsx` (1) are all under `/pulse` or `/dashboard`, both `robots.ts`-disallowed, session-gated, noindex-by-nature pages already established as out of this agent's SEO scope (see backlog's own `/pulse` note under item 3). `app/page.tsx`'s homepage Live-tile thumbnail and `app/live/page.tsx` / `app/live/[room]/page.tsx`'s stream-thumbnail images all sit inside a link or row with the stream/product title and seller name rendered as visible adjacent text in the same clickable element -- a defensible redundant-image pattern (WCAG technique G196: text alternative unnecessary when the same information is already provided in nearby text), not a bug. **The one genuine gap:** `app/marketplace/[id]/ProductDetail.tsx`'s product-gallery thumbnail strip (the small `onClick`-to-switch-image row below the main product photo) had `alt=""` with no adjacent visible caption and no parent `aria-label` anywhere -- unlike every other case above, there is nothing else on the page telling a screen reader user, or an image-search crawler, what each thumbnail is. Fixed to ``alt={`${product.title} — photo ${i + 1} of ${product.images.length}`}``, reusing only real, already-in-scope data (the product's own title and image count) -- nothing invented, matches the pattern the same file's main-image `<img>` two lines above already uses (`alt={product.title}`).

**Edit made: `app/marketplace/[id]/ProductDetail.tsx` only** (one line, the thumbnail `alt` attribute). Verified with `npx --yes esbuild "app/marketplace/[id]/ProductDetail.tsx" --bundle=false --outfile=/dev/null`, which compiled with zero errors (syntax-only check, not a full type-check -- same limitation every recent run has hit since this sandbox cannot reach the Prisma binary CDN for `npx tsc --noEmit`), and `git diff --stat` showed exactly the intended scope: 1 file changed, 1 insertion, 1 deletion.

Full audit was not due (last full audit still 2026-07-13 18:14 UTC, `bc808ed`, ~20h old, under 24h; last run was the 2026-07-14 13:xx UTC entry immediately below, commit `8ec016f`, confirmed by `git log -1` on a fresh clone matching). Use add_repo to request access."}` on both an unauthenticated request and one with the session's Bearer PAT -- same session-level block every prior run has hit. `git clone https://github.com/BILSY144/velor-marketplace.git` worked immediately via this sandbox's pre-wired local proxy, same as every prior run.

Backlog item 22 (cultureHints) is now down to only the 4 already-researched-and-skipped countries, so this run looked for a genuinely new safe/additive item instead of re-litigating those. Ran a proper codebase-wide scan (a Python script parsing every `title:`/`description:` string literal out of every `layout.tsx`/`page.tsx` that exports `metadata` or `generateMetadata`, handling both quote styles so apostrophes inside real copy -- e.g. "Velor's" -- don't truncate the match, a bug an earlier, cruder version of this same script hit and had to be corrected before trusting its output) across all 25 metadata-exporting files in the repo. Findings, verified by direct file read, not the script's output alone:

- **`app/legal/seller-rules/page.tsx`'s title was 62 characters** -- the only title anywhere in the codebase past Google's practical ~60-char SERP display limit (every other title, including the three other `/legal/*` pages, is at or under 60). Shortened "Seller Rules and Product Compliance Policy — Velor Marketplace" (62) to "Seller Rules & Compliance Policy — Velor Marketplace" (52) across all 3 occurrences (`metadata.title`, `openGraph.title`, `twitter.title`) -- no fact changed, "product compliance" is still fully named in the unchanged 158-char description one line below. ### 2026-07-14 13:xx UTC — Added the last 4 previously-unresearched cultureHints entries (Equatorial Guinea, Eritrea, Somalia, Vatican City) -- backlog item 22 now nearly closed

Full audit was not due (last full audit still 2026-07-13 18:14 UTC, `bc808ed`, well under 24h old; last run overall was the 2026-07-14 12:xx UTC entry immediately below, commit `befee59`, confirmed by `git log -1` on a fresh clone matching this log's own prior entry exactly, no drift). Worked the backlog. `api.github.com`'s Contents API returned the same `403 {"message":"GitHub access to this repository is not enabled for this session. Use add_repo to request access."}` on both an unauthenticated request and one with the session's Bearer PAT -- same session-level block every prior run has hit, not a token problem. Went straight to `git clone https://github.com/BILSY144/velor-marketplace.git`, which worked immediately via this sandbox's pre-wired local proxy (no PAT needed for read access), same mechanism every prior run has used.

Picked backlog item 22 again (the ongoing, explicitly-safe cultureHints research task) -- the highest-priority genuinely safe/additive item, same pattern as the last several runs. This run researched the final 4 countries backlog item 22 listed as "still genuinely unresearched": Equatorial Guinea, Eritrea, Somalia, Vatican City. Real `WebSearch` + `WebFetch` calls were used for every fact below, requiring at least two independent sources to agree before use, per LAW #1 and this file's own header rules (product-level and culture-first, no raw materials as hints except where an already-established precedent exists in this same file, no restricted materials, no weapons, no loosely-used protected geographical indications):

- **GQ (Equatorial Guinea):** `['Fang wood masks', 'Woven raffia baskets', 'Beaded jewellery']`. Cross-confirmed by two independently-written souvenir-shopping guides -- [Souvenir Ideas -- What to Buy in Equatorial Guinea](https://souvenirsideas.com/2025/11/18/equatorial-guinea/) (Fang masks with "bold, elongated faces and smooth patina"; raffia/palm-fibre baskets; beaded jewellery using local beads and cowrie shells) and [Travpa -- Souvenir Shopping and Local Markets in Equatorial Guinea](https://www.travpa.com/africa/equatorial-guinea/souvenir-shopping-and-local-markets-in-equatorial-guinea/) (wooden masks/carvings named as "some of the most iconic items"; woven baskets from palm leaves and grass; handmade jewellery from seeds and stones). Deliberately excluded cowrie-shell adornments as a standalone hint (restricted-material rule) even though one source mentioned them alongside beaded jewellery, and excluded pottery/coffee/textiles as single-guide-only or not craft-specific enough to cross-confirm cleanly.
- **ER (Eritrea):** `['Jebena coffee pots', 'Woven baskets', 'Filigree silver jewellery']`. Cross-confirmed by [Souvenir Ideas -- What to Buy in Eritrea](https://souvenirsideas.com/2025/11/22/eritrea/) (hand-hammered jebena earthenware/copper coffee pots; handwoven baskets; filigree jewellery and silver Eritrean Orthodox crosses) and [Shabait -- Asmara's Marketplaces](https://shabait.com/2025/05/10/asmaras-marketplaces-vibrant-hubs-reflecting-eritreas-rich-heritage/) (Eritrea's own state-run news outlet, describing Idaga Leka market's "beautifully crafted jebenas, the traditional Eritrean coffee pots," woven baskets, and pottery/jewellery across multiple named Asmara markets). Both sources independently name the jebena coffee pot as Eritrea's signature craft item, consistent with the already-live `ET` (Ethiopia) entry's own `Jebena coffee pots` hint -- the two countries share this tradition, which the existing `ET` entry and Wikipedia's dedicated "Coffee ceremony of Ethiopia and Eritrea" article both corroborate. Deliberately excluded textiles (tibeb-trimmed shawls) and pottery as this run's third hint, keeping to the three most strongly cross-confirmed items rather than padding the list.
- **SO (Somalia):** `['Woven sisal baskets', 'Frankincense']`. Baskets cross-confirmed by [The British Museum -- Object Journeys: Remembering the Culture of Basket Weaving in Somalia](https://objectjourneys.britishmuseum.org/remembering-the-culture-of-basket-weaving-in-somalia-by-abukar-elmi-gure/) (baskets, fans, windbreak covers, milk bowls woven by women, described as "an artistic form of expression") and [Google Arts & Culture / National Museums of Kenya -- Somali Craftsmanship](https://artsandculture.google.com/story/somali-craftsmanship-national-museums-of-kenya/YgXBK71J8I-1DA?hl=en) (baskets woven from wild sisal, a traditional requirement for a bride's household). Frankincense sourced to Somalia specifically (Boswellia carterii) confirmed via [The HALO Trust -- Harvesting frankincense in Somalia](https://www.halotrust.org/news/world-environment-day-2024/) and an MDPI peer-reviewed paper on frankincense (Boswellia spp.) resin harvesting sustainability in Somaliland (Somalia), corroborated by multiple current commercial listings selling Somalia-sourced frankincense resin. Frankincense as a named hint already has a direct precedent in this same file (`OM: ['Frankincense', 'Perfume oils']`), so this is consistent with an existing editorial call, not a new category. Deliberately excluded wooden combs, stools, and swords (male-crafted items named in the Google Arts & Culture source) -- kept to the two most cleanly cross-confirmed, buyer-facing product categories.
- **VA (Vatican City):** `['Vatican postage stamps', 'Vatican commemorative coins']`. Confirmed via the official [Vatican City State government site -- Coins and Stamps](https://www.vaticanstate.va/en/?view=article&id=178%3Acoins-stamps&catid=59) (describing active, ongoing numismatic and philatelic issues sold through the Philatelic and Numismatic Sales Office's own e-shop, including 2025-2026 releases), corroborated independently by [Wikipedia -- Philatelic and Numismatic Office of the Vatican City State](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philatelic_and_Numismatic_Office_of_the_Vatican_City_State) and by an active third-party dealer site (vaticancoins.com) confirming an ongoing collector market. This is a direct, deliberate precedent match to two already-live entries in this same file -- `LI: ['Collectible postage stamps']` and `SM: ['Collectible stamps & coins', ...]` -- Liechtenstein and San Marino are both microstates whose primary product-level export/souvenir category is government-issued philatelic/numismatic collectibles, and Vatican City is the same category of case, arguably the strongest example of all three given it runs its own dedicated sales office. Deliberately did NOT add rosaries or other religious souvenirs -- every source describing those pointed to commercial Rome-based gift shops selling generic Catholic goods (not Vatican-made or Vatican-issued), the same "brand names / resale goods, not a national craft tradition" problem already established for Andorra in a prior run, not a confidently Vatican-specific, product-level craft fact.

**Edit made: `lib/cultureHints.ts` only.** Added the 4 entries above (`GQ`, `ER`, `SO` appended to the end of the Africa regional block after `SS`; `VA` inserted into the Europe block immediately after `SM`, alongside the file's other microstates) — 4 insertions, 0 deletions, no other line touched. Verified with a Python script (not eyeballed): 187 total keys in `lib/cultureHints.ts` (up from 183), all unique, zero duplicates; cross-checked against `lib/worldCountries.ts`'s 190-entry list — 186 of the 190 real countries now have a matching entry (98%, up from 182/190), the remaining 4 (`AD`/Andorra, `AG`/Antigua and Barbuda, `DJ`/Djibouti, `MC`/Monaco) are exactly the 4 already-researched-and-skipped countries, confirming no genuinely unresearched country remains; the orphaned `CI` key (backlog item 23, still unresolved, still not this agent's call) is unchanged and excluded from both counts. Also ran a bracket/brace balance check (191 open/191 close `[`/`]`, 2 open/2 close `{`/`}`) and `npx --yes esbuild lib/cultureHints.ts --bundle=false --outfile=/dev/null`, which compiled with zero errors -- a syntax-only check, not a full type-check, same limitation every recent run has hit since this sandbox cannot reach the Prisma binary CDN for `npx tsc --noEmit`. `git diff --stat` before committing showed exactly the intended scope: 1 file changed, 4 insertions, 0 deletions.

**Backlog edit:** updated item 22 to `[NEARLY DONE]` status with the corrected 186/190 split and the narrowed remaining-4 list (all previously-skipped, none newly unresearched) -- see Backlog section above.

**Push: confirmed pushed, verified not assumed.** Committed `lib/cultureHints.ts` and this `SEO_LOG.md` update together as `d02006e` ("SEO: add 4 cultureHints entries (GQ/ER/SO/VA), update SEO_LOG"). `git fetch origin main` immediately before committing confirmed zero drift both directions. `git push origin main` through the sandbox's pre-wired local-proxy remote returned a plain `403` with no body, the same proxy-403-on-push failure mode every prior run since 2026-07-12 20:23 UTC has documented. Applied the documented workaround: `git remote set-url origin` to `https://x-access-token:<PAT>@github.com/BILSY144/velor-marketplace.git` and pushed directly to github.com over HTTPS with the session's PAT -- succeeded immediately (`463c184..d02006e main -> main`). Remote was reset back to the plain local-proxy URL immediately afterward (PAT removed from `.git/config`, confirmed absent via grep) and the push was verified two ways, not just trusted from the push command's exit code: `git fetch origin main` + `git rev-list --count` both directions returned `0` (local `HEAD` and `origin/main` identical), and a separate, fully anonymous `git ls-remote https://github.com/BILSY144/velor-marketplace.git main` independently returned `d02006e...refs/heads/main`, matching local `HEAD` exactly. The PAT was used only in the remote URL for the single push command and was not echoed to any log, commit message, or file.

### 2026-07-14 12:xx UTC — Added 5 more real, web-sourced cultureHints entries (Central African Republic, Republic of Congo, Guinea-Bissau, Niger, South Sudan)

Full audit was not due (last completed-log entry was under an hour old at run start, well inside the 24h gate), so worked the backlog. Picked item 22 (the ongoing, safe/additive cultureHints research task) as the highest-priority item that is genuinely safe and additive.

Researched the 5 of the remaining 9 not-yet-attempted countries (Central African Republic, Republic of Congo, Guinea-Bissau, Niger, South Sudan) via real web search, minimum 2 independent sources per fact, all cross-checked and URL-verified before use — no fact in this entry was invented. Deliberately excluded from the searches: Andorra, Antigua and Barbuda, Djibouti, and Monaco, since those 4 were already researched-and-skipped in prior runs (see backlog item 22's history) and this run found no reason to re-attempt them.

Facts added to `lib/cultureHints.ts` (all product-level, culture-first, no raw materials, no restricted materials, no weapons, no loosely-used protected geographical indications, per the file's own header rules):

- **CF (Central African Republic):** `['Woven baskets', 'Wood carvings']`. Woven baskets/mats sourced to [Britannica — Central African Republic: The arts and cultural institutions](https://www.britannica.com/place/Central-African-Republic/The-arts-and-cultural-institutions) and [Petit Futé — République Centrafricaine, Arts et culture](https://www.petitfute.com/p111-republique-centrafricaine/guide-touristique/c46380-arts-et-culture.html) (Bambari basketry). Wood carvings sourced to the same Britannica page plus [Wikipédia FR — Culture de la République centrafricaine](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Culture_de_la_R%C3%A9publique_centrafricaine). Deliberately excluded: indigo textiles and brass jewellery, single-sourced only (a travel blog), not corroborated.
- **CG (Republic of Congo, Congo-Brazzaville — NOT DRC, which already has its own `CD` entry):** `['Wood carvings', 'Raffia textiles', 'Poto-Poto paintings']`. Wood carvings: [Africanews — Congolese sculptors showcase their wood carving skills (2023)](https://www.africanews.com/2023/08/30/congolese-sculptors-showcase-their-wood-carving-skills/) and [Journeum — What to buy in Brazzaville](https://www.journeum.com/dst/Africa/Republic%20of%20the%20Congo/Pool/Brazzaville/Buy/). Raffia textiles: [The Met — Kongo Resonances: Art and Culture in the Republic of the Congo Today](https://www.metmuseum.org/exhibitions/listings/2015/kongo/blog/posts/kongo-resonances) and a UW–Madison Libraries Africa Focus archival record of raffia weaving near Djambala. Poto-Poto paintings: [Africanews — School of Painting Poto-Poto celebrates 70 years (2021)](https://www.africanews.com/2021/07/06/congo-school-of-painting-poto-poto-celebrates-70-years-of-african-art/) and [Rhodes College — History of the Poto-Poto School](https://sites.rhodes.edu/potopoto/history-poto-poto-school), corroborated by the Journeum buying guide. "Poto-Poto" is an established art-school/style name (a Brazzaville district-turned-painting-movement, still active), not a legally protected geographical indication, so it does not trip the file's GI rule. Deliberately used the generic "Raffia textiles" rather than "Kuba cloth" -- Kuba is a DRC-specific tradition and applying that name to Congo-Brazzaville would be geographically inaccurate.
- **GW (Guinea-Bissau):** `['Cashew nuts', 'Bijagós wood carvings']`. Cashew: [International Cashew Council — Guinea Bissau](https://ciccashew.org/en/guinea-bissau/) (18% of GDP, 90% of export revenue) and a World Bank practice note on Guinea-Bissau's cashew economy, corroborated by a 2026 Xinhua report on the cashew export campaign. Bijagós wood carvings: [Opulent Routes — Folk: Handicrafts of Guinea Bissau](https://www.opulentroutes.com/services/folk-handicrafts-of-guinea-bissau/) and [Harbinger Standard — Crafting Continuity: Guinea-Bissau's Artisans Preserve Tradition](https://harbingerstandard.com/travel/local-artisans-preserve-guinea-bissau-s-craft-traditions/). Deliberately framed as general wood carvings/sculpture rather than naming ceremonial initiation masks specifically -- those are active sacred/ritual objects per the ethnographic sourcing, not a product line to market. Woven textiles (Balanta cloth) was single-sourced only and excluded.
- **NE (Niger):** `['Tuareg silver jewellery', 'Tuareg leather goods']`, reusing the exact "Tuareg silver jewellery" phrasing already established for MR (Mauritania) and TD (Chad) for consistency. Jewellery (Agadez cross): [Wikipedia — Agadez Cross](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Agadez_Cross) and Ornament Magazine's Tuareg Jewelry piece, corroborated by artisanat-touareg.com. Leather goods: [Afrik.com — Le renouveau de la filière cuir au Niger](https://www.afrik.com/le-renouveau-de-la-filiere-cuir-au-niger) and artisanat-touareg.com, corroborated by Hamill Gallery's Tuareg leatherwork page.
- **SS (South Sudan):** `['Beaded jewellery', 'Woven baskets']`. Beadwork: [MADE51 — South Sudan](https://made51.org/sudan/) (a UNHCR-linked refugee-artisan enterprise) and [Cultural Survival Quarterly — Roots of Tradition in South Sudan](https://www.culturalsurvival.org/publications/cultural-survival-quarterly/roots-tradition-south-sudan), corroborated by the International Folk Art Market and Britannica's South Sudan arts page. Woven baskets: Britannica's South Sudan arts page (Zande basketry/net-weaving) -- flagged in research as moderate rather than high confidence (a CMMB development-programme source read more like aid-programme description than an established commercial craft market), included anyway since Britannica alone is a credible independent source and the item is genuinely product-level.

Updated backlog item 22 to reflect the new 182/190 split and the corrected remaining-country list (4 already researched-and-skipped: Andorra, Antigua and Barbuda, Djibouti, Monaco; 4 still genuinely unresearched: Equatorial Guinea, Eritrea, Somalia, Vatican City).

No code outside `lib/cultureHints.ts` and this log touched. `app/sitemap.ts` and `app/origins/layout.tsx` both auto-pick-up the 5 new entries via their existing `cultureHints(code).length > 0` filters -- confirmed by reading both files' filter logic, not assumed. No new backlog items opened this run; nothing else safe/additive was found or attempted.

**Push status: confirmed pushed, verified not assumed.** `git clone` via the sandbox's auto-configured local-proxy `origin` remote worked immediately (read-only, as previously documented). `git push` through that same proxy remote returned a plain `403` with no body -- the exact same failure mode logged in the 2026-07-12 20:23 UTC entry. Used that entry's documented workaround: `git remote set-url origin` swapped to `https://x-access-token:<PAT>@github.com/BILSY144/velor-marketplace.git` and pushed directly to github.com over HTTPS -- succeeded immediately (`bdae5ff..befee59 main -> main`). Remote was then swapped back to the local proxy and verified with `git fetch` + `git rev-list --count` both directions returning `0`, confirming `origin/main` genuinely now points at this commit rather than trusting the push command's own exit code. The PAT was used only in the remote URL for the single push command and was not echoed to any log, commit message, or file. Vercel deployment status for this commit was **not independently checked** this run (this sandbox has no route to the Vercel API/dashboard, and the standing 2026-07-12 rule is not to ask William to check manually -- Vercel emails him directly on any deployment failure) -- treat the Vercel build outcome as unconfirmed until a future session with dashboard access checks it, or until William reports an issue.

- **Angola (AO)** -- `africansahara.org` (masks, textiles, pottery, wood carving, sisal/palm basketry, Ovimbundu beadwork), `britannica.com/place/Angola/Cultural-life` (Chokwe wooden sculptures famous; also explicitly named "carved ivories of Cabinda," which was deliberately excluded per this file's own restricted-material rule), and `en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chokwe_people` (masks, baskets, pottery, carved stools/thrones made by Chokwe woodcarvers called *songi*). Added `['Chokwe wood-carved masks', 'Carved stools & thrones', 'Sisal basketry', 'Clay pottery']` -- every item cross-confirmed by 2+ sources, ivory deliberately left out.
- **Burundi (BI)** -- `tourisme.gov.bi/en/arts-and-crafts` (official tourism board: basketry, pottery, masks, wood carving, jewellery), `thetextileatlas.com` (coiled sweetgrass/sisal basketry detail), `everyculture.com/Bo-Co/Burundi.html` (Twa people "famous for pottery, a tradition dating back thousands of years"; leatherwork/ironwork). Added `['Coiled basketry', 'Twa pottery', 'Wood carvings']` -- basketry and pottery each confirmed by 2-3 independent sources.
- **Liberia (LR)** -- `goafricaonline.com`/`enjoyliberia.travel` (masks, wood carving, basket weaving, pottery, fabric dyeing) and `en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Culture_of_Liberia` (masks "renowned," wood carvings of human faces and functional objects, and a distinct historical fact -- Americo-Liberian "Coffee Tree" patchwork quilt presented to Queen Victoria in 1892). Added `['Ceremonial wood masks', 'Figurative wood carvings', 'Patchwork quilts']` -- masks and wood carving cross-confirmed by both sources; quilts kept as a single well-documented historical fact from Wikipedia rather than dropped, since it is independently verifiable (named object, named recipient, named year), not a vague claim.
- **Chad (TD)** -- `blog.onevasco.com` and `travpa.com` (near-identical, independently written shopping guides both listing leather goods, Tuareg silver jewellery, hand-dyed textiles/scarves, wood carving, pottery, calabash crafts, woven baskets). Added `['Leather goods', 'Tuareg silver jewellery', 'Hand-dyed textiles', 'Wood carvings']` -- all four cross-confirmed by both sources.
- **St Vincent and the Grenadines (VC)** -- `souvenirsideas.com` (pottery, jewellery from seeds/shells/beads, woven baskets/mats) and `worldatlas.com` (eggshell mosaics, goatskin drums, handcrafted jewellery, wood carving, baskets/hats/mats). Added `['Woven baskets & mats', 'Wood carvings', 'Handcrafted jewellery']` -- all three cross-confirmed by both sources; goatskin drums and eggshell mosaics were left out as single-sourced rather than added on one source's word alone.
