- **Independently cross-checked the file's own internal consistency with a Python script** (not eyeballed) before choosing new countries: compared every code in `lib/worldCountries.ts`'s 190-entry `WORLD_COUNTRIES` array against every key in `lib/cultureHints.ts`. Confirmed the prior run's numbers exactly -- 169 unique keys (including the still-orphaned `CI`), 22 real `WORLD_COUNTRIES` codes without a matching entry, list matching the prior entry's exactly.
- Chose 6 of the 22 missing countries to research, favouring ones with strong, easily-independently-verifiable single-topic product facts, and ran real `WebSearch` (plus `WebFetch` on the strongest source for each, to confirm the product/business is real and current, not just historical) before writing anything, per LAW #1 and this log's own standing rule against fabricating country content. 4 of the 6 produced confidently verifiable, product-level facts; 2 did not and were deliberately dropped rather than stretched:
  - **Luxembourg (LU):** Péckvillercher clay bird whistles -- confirmed via Luxembourg's own official tourism site (visitluxembourg.com, "Local products" page), corroborated independently by Wikipedia's "Emaischen" article and Delano News (a real Luxembourg English-language news outlet, "Péckvillercher market to take place in Luxembourg City, Nospelt") -- these whistles are sold every Easter Monday at the Émaischen pottery-and-craft market in Luxembourg City and Nospelt, a real, currently-running annual tradition, not a historical one.
  - **Gabon (GA):** Mbigou stone carvings -- confirmed via TRT Afrika, naming the stone specifically ("Mbigou stone," white/grayish-green/brown, valued by weight) and describing a real, currently-working artisan (26-year-old Cyril Biyoghe in Libreville, earning $100-200 per sculpture from tourist buyers) carving it into human/animal figures, masks, and scenes -- a currently-active commercial craft, not historical-only. Deliberately did not use Fang masks/wood carving as a second Gabon item -- initial search results conflated Fang ceremonial masks with a broader "African masks" Etsy category without a Gabon-specific, currently-sold sourcing chain as clean as the Mbigou-stone one; left for a future run rather than added on weaker confidence.
  - **Mauritania (MR):** Tuareg silver jewellery, Malahfa embroidered textiles -- confirmed via a souvenir-guide feature (souvenirsideas.com) naming both specifically (silver jewellery with Tuareg-cross designs; malahfa, the traditional embroidered women's fabric) and citing Mauritania's own National Office of Mauritanian Handicrafts and the Capital Market in Nouakchott as real sales channels; corroborated independently via multiple live Etsy "Mauritania jewelry" / "Mauritanian jewelry" search results showing the same product category is actively sold today, not just described.
  - **Tuvalu (TV):** Pandanus & coconut-leaf weaving, Kolose crochet -- confirmed via Atlas Obscura's feature on the real, visitable Tuvalu Women's Handicraft Centre in Funafuti (baskets, fans, mats, fishhooks and woodcarvings made from coconut palm, pandanus leaf and hibiscus) and independently via a Google Arts & Culture feature on kolose crochet, confirming it remains actively practised today (the Fafine Niutao I Aotearoa artisan collective still meets weekly; a 2018 kolose-panelled dress was displayed at Buckingham Palace as part of the Commonwealth Fashion Exchange) -- both a real historical craft and a currently commercially made one.
  - **Andorra -- researched, deliberately NOT added.** Fetched Andorra's own official tourism site (visitandorra.com, "Local products" page) directly. Every named product was a gourmet food/drink producer brand (cured meats, cheeses, honey, craft beer, wine, a herbal liqueur) rather than a culture-first craft item in the style this file's own header rule favours ("Porcelain tea sets," not a brand name); the one textile mention ("Vital -- wool blankets, pillows, bags") was a single brand name, not established as a broader national craft tradition the way e.g. Andean or Basotho weaving is for other countries. No confidently product-level, non-brand-name craft fact turned up this run -- logged here rather than silently dropped or stretched into a weak entry.
  - **Djibouti -- researched, deliberately NOT added.** The one specific, well-described product lead found (hornheritage.org: cowrie-shell-decorated ceremonial milk vessels and shields) is exactly the restricted-material category this file's own header rule excludes ("no restricted-material products (ivory, coral, shell) as hints"), so it could not be used regardless of source quality. The other lead (harbingerstandard.com: woven palm-fibre baskets, an embroidered "Gadha" shawl, hand-painted pottery) rested on a single source this run doesn't have independent confidence in -- a second attempt to reach Aid to Artisans' own Djibouti feature (ata.creativelearning.org) hit a redirect loop and could not be read, and mapsofworld.com returned a 403. Left open for a future run to try different sourcing rather than added on one uncorroborated source.
- **Edit made: `lib/cultureHints.ts`.** Added the 4 entries above (`LU`, `GA`, `MR`, `TV`) in their correct regional groupings (Benelux/Alpine Europe, Central Africa, West/Saharan Africa, Pacific/Oceania), keeping the file's existing product-level phrasing convention and the header's rule against raw materials or restricted-material hints. Verified with a script: 173 total keys, all unique, no duplicates (up from 169); 172 of the 190 real `WORLD_COUNTRIES` entries now have a matching entry (90%), 18 do not (the orphaned `CI` key still excluded from both counts, unchanged, still backlog item 23).
- **Edit made: `app/sitemap.ts`.** Added a new dated comment block (2026-07-14, 10:xx UTC run) rather than editing the prior 09:xx-UTC-run paragraph, same "don't silently rewrite history" approach every recent run has used -- records the corrected 172/190 split, the exact remaining-18-country list, the Andorra/Djibouti research-and-skip notes, and reconfirms the still-unresolved `CI`/Côte d'Ivoire orphan (backlog item 23) is unaffected by this run's edits. No functional code change -- the filter already re-derives from `cultureHints()` and `WORLD_COUNTRIES` directly, so the 4 new countries are picked up automatically by both `app/sitemap.ts`'s `/origins/[slug]` entries and `app/origins/layout.tsx`'s `ItemList` JSON-LD with no further code change.
- **Backlog edits:** updated item 22 with the corrected numbers (18 remaining, not 22), the new remaining-18 list, and the Andorra/Djibouti research notes. Item 23 (orphaned `CI` key) left unchanged -- still William's call, not touched or re-investigated this run.

Re-ran the Python cross-check script after the edit (not trusting hand arithmetic) to confirm the real numbers used in this entry and the new comment: 173 total keys / 172 real-country matches / 18 remaining, exact list, zero duplicate keys.

**Push:** `git fetch origin main` immediately before committing confirmed `git rev-list --count` returned `0` both directions -- no drift, no rebase needed. Committed as `4b315b3`. `git push origin main` through the plain, pre-wired local-proxy remote (no PAT set) returned a `403` with no body, the same proxy-403-on-push several earlier runs (e.g. 2026-07-12 20:23 UTC) hit and documented a workaround for. Applied that documented workaround: `git remote set-url origin "https://x-access-token:<PAT>@github.com/BILSY144/velor-marketplace.git"` and pushed directly to github.com over HTTPS with the PAT -- succeeded immediately (`5793b1e..4b315b3 main -> main`, no 403). **Confirmed pushed at the git-protocol level, not just trusting the push command's exit code:** ran `git ls-remote https://github.com/BILSY144/velor-marketplace.git main` directly afterward -- returned `4b315b3527bcc86fdab03b6eede6799d876bf33f refs/heads/main`, matching local `HEAD` exactly. The remote URL was reset back to the plain proxy remote (PAT removed from local git config) immediately after confirming the push, per the standing instruction to treat the PAT as sensitive and not leave it sitting in config. This log update is a separate, second commit pushed immediately after, referencing this real code commit's real SHA (`4b315b3`).

**Vercel deployment:** not independently checked this run, per this log's own standing rule (Vercel auto-notifies William directly, by web and email, on any deployment failure, and this sandbox has no general outbound network access beyond `github.com` regardless).   - **Cape Verde (CV):** Grogue sugarcane spirit, handwoven baskets -- confirmed via a souvenir-guide feature (paulinaontheroad.com) listing grogue (a spirit distilled on Santo Antão from sugarcane) and locally woven baskets among the country's real, currently-sold local goods; corroborated by a second souvenir-guide search result (souvenirsideas.com) surfacing the same items independently.
  - **Bahamas (BS):** Androsia hand-batiked textiles, straw-woven goods -- confirmed via Androsia's own operating business site (androsia.com; est. 1960 on Andros Island by the Birch family, still active today with a working factory, phone number, and online shop selling hand-batiked fabric, clothing/accessories, and home-line goods) and independently via bahamas.com's own tourism-board page on the same factory; straw-woven goods confirmed via multiple independent sources on Nassau's real, currently-operating Straw Market.
  - **Barbados (BB):** Chalky Mount pottery, rum cakes, mahogany carvings -- confirmed via barbados.org's crafts page, which names Chalky Mount (East Coast) specifically as a real, centuries-old pottery-production area making Caribbean blue/green tableware, vases, and decorative pieces; rum cakes and mahogany carvings ("raw wood transformed by local artists ... functional pieces") confirmed on the same page as real craft-market goods, not raw materials.
  - **Liechtenstein (LI):** Collectible postage stamps -- confirmed via Liechtenstein Marketing's own official site (en.liechtenstein-marketing.li), which frames stamps as "cultural ambassadors for the country" issued as a deliberate symbol of national identity since Liechtenstein began issuing independently, and confirms production is still active today (two-to-three-year design cycles, recent examples including an NFT stamp with artist Romero Britto and a 2023 joint issue with Switzerland) -- corroborated by ÖSVLPH's own September 2025 new-issues bulletin, confirming stamps are still being freshly issued as of last year, not a purely historical craft.
  - **Macau (MO):** Macau almond cookies, Portuguese-style egg tarts -- confirmed via Koi Kei Bakery's own site (a real, currently operating bakery chain) and independently via Tasteatlas, which names Macau almond cookies as "the primary souvenir of choice for tourists" and egg tarts as an essential Macau bakery item; deliberately phrased "Portuguese-style egg tarts" rather than a bare "Egg tarts" since the dish's Portuguese-colonial origin (via Macau's own Lord Stow's Bakery adaptation) is part of what makes it a distinctly Macanese product rather than a generic pastry.
  - **San Marino (SM):** Collectible stamps & coins, Torta Tre Monti wafer cake -- confirmed via a dedicated Italian travel-guide feature (ita.travel) naming San Marino's own postage stamps and coins as real philatelic/numismatic collector products issued since the 19th century, and Torta Tre Monti as a specific, named traditional Sammarinese cake (wafer layers bound with chocolate or hazelnut cream) sold as a real food product, not invented.

- Chose 6 of the 28 missing countries, favouring ones with strong, easily-independently-verifiable single-topic product facts, and ran real `WebSearch` (plus `WebFetch` on the strongest source for each, to pull concrete product names and confirm the craft is still actively made/sold today, not just historical) before writing anything, per LAW #1 and this log's own standing rule against fabricating country content:
  - **Benin (BJ):** Abomey appliqué tapestries -- confirmed via Wikipedia's own Royal Palaces of Abomey article (UNESCO World Heritage Site, capital of the former Kingdom of Dahomey) and a dedicated African-appliqué feature (nomad4now.com) describing the craftsmen's guild tradition of appliquéd wall hangings depicting royal history; corroborated as still commercially sold today via a "What to Buy in Benin" souvenir guide and live Etsy listings under "Abomey Applique." **Deliberately did not use "bronze/brass casting"** despite it surfacing prominently in initial searches -- verified via multiple sources (Wikipedia, British Museum, Met Museum) that the famous "Benin Bronzes" are from the historical Kingdom of Benin, centred on Benin City, which is in Edo State, Nigeria today, an entirely different kingdom from the modern Republic of Benin (former Dahomey) despite the name overlap. Attributing the Benin Bronzes to the country of Benin would have been a real factual error -- caught and avoided this run, not just assumed correct from the name.
  - **Gambia (GM):** Serekunda batik textiles -- confirmed via an AccessGambia feature on the Serrekunda Batik Factory (a named, real operation supplying "many of the local stalls and the tourist market"), cross-checked against an Ancient Origins video feature on a Gambian batik artisan and a Tripadvisor listing confirming the factory is a currently visitable, operating attraction.
  - **Guyana (GY):** Tibiseri straw baskets -- confirmed via a Guyana Chronicle feature on tibiseri (palm-leaf) straw craft describing the full preparation process and finished products (bags, skirts, mats, bowls, hammocks), tied to Amerindian Heritage Month and sold through the Ministry of Amerindian Affairs Craft Shop. **Deliberately did not add balata craft** (carved-latex figurines/souvenirs) as a second item -- a dedicated source on the topic confirmed the commercial balata industry ceased operations in 1982 and could not confirm current active production beyond one photo caption of a single Rupununi craft shop; not confident enough to include, so left out rather than stretched.
  - **Dominica (DM):** Kalinago larouma-reed baskets, Carved calabash art -- both confirmed via the Kalinago people's own archive site (kalinagoarchive.org), which names larouma reed products specifically (baskets, fans, armbands, mats, finger traps, natural mud/plant dyes) and calabash gourd carving/engraving as two distinct, ongoing craft industries providing "seasonal employment for artisans in the Kalinago Territory" today.
  - **St Kitts and Nevis (KN):** Caribelle batik textiles -- confirmed via Caribelle Batik's own site (Romney Manor location), a named, currently operating business (est. 1975, celebrating its 50th anniversary in 2025, 36 local staff, active retail and wholesale), using traditional Indonesian wax-resist batik methods.
  - **Kiribati (KI):** Pandanus-leaf woven mats -- confirmed via Kiribati's own official tourism-board site (kiribatitourism.gov.ki) naming mat weaving among the country's core traditional craft skills, corroborated by Creative NZ and Te Papa Tongarewa (New Zealand's national museum) collection references to Kiribati weaving traditions.
  - **Antigua and Barbuda -- researched, deliberately NOT added.** Searched for a craft-heritage angle; the strongest, most-repeated result (Betty's Hope) is a historic sugar-plantation and windmill site, not a craft or product -- a tourism/history attraction, not something a buyer would shop for. No confidently product-level, well-sourced craft fact turned up in this run's search. Logged here rather than silently dropped, so a future run knows this country was tried and came up short, not simply unattempted.

Found `CI` (Cacao, Wax-print fashion) is a real, well-formed cultureHints entry with no matching country anywhere in `worldCountries.ts` -- confirmed by dumping and reading all 190 country names directly, not just a regex miss (no "Ivoire" or "Ivory" substring anywhere in the file). This means Côte d'Ivoire is missing from the site's country list entirely, not just missing a cultureHints entry. Logged as new backlog item 23 rather than fixed -- adding a 191st country touches the "190 countries" copy on `/apply`/`/founding`, a bigger call than this agent's additive lane (see backlog item 23 for full reasoning). This also means the prior run's "145/190" and "151/190" framing, and this run's own "40 remaining" starting point, were always counting against 190 real `WORLD_COUNTRIES` entries correctly (the orphaned `CI` key was never counted as one of the "with entry" 145/151, since those counts came from filtering `WORLD_COUNTRIES` first) -- no prior entry was inaccurate, this is a new finding, not a correction of one.
- Chose 6 of the then-40 countries without a cultureHints entry, favouring ones with strong, easily-independently-verifiable single-topic facts, and ran a real `WebSearch` (plus `WebFetch` on the strongest source for each, to pull concrete product names rather than relying on search snippets) before writing anything, per LAW #1 and this log's own standing rule against fabricating country content:
  - **Turkmenistan (TM):** Hand-knotted Turkmen carpets -- confirmed via Wikipedia's own Turkmen rug article and UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage listing ("Traditional Turkmen carpet making art," inscribed 2019); carpet motifs appear on Turkmenistan's national flag and emblem, and the country has a national "Turkmen Carpet Day" holiday, underscoring this is a genuinely well-documented national craft, not a minor sideline. Deliberately added only this one item -- did not stretch to a second, less-verifiable product.
  - **Malawi (MW):** Dedza pottery, Chitenje wax-print textiles, Carved wood figures -- confirmed via a souvenir-guide source naming Dedza (hand-thrown pottery with cream/brown/olive glazes), chitenje (wax-print fabric worn as wraps and headscarves), and wood carvings from Mua Cultural Centre and Mulanje (mvule and rosewood). Deliberately left out that same source's "carved fish sculptures" and "Gule Wamkulu masks" (thinner, single-source claims) and did not use "wood carvings" alone since the source names a specific, verifiable production centre.
  - **Togo (TG):** Kente cloth, Batik textiles -- kente confirmed via an OkayAfrica feature specifically on kente weaving in Togo (not just Ghana, where kente is more commonly associated); batik confirmed via a fabric-trade source (True Fabrics) describing batik-technique cotton fabrics from Togo.
  - **Sierra Leone (SL):** Gara tie-dye textiles -- confirmed via Sierra Leone's own tourism board (visitsierraleone.org) and an Africanews piece on the craft's history and modern revival. Deliberately added only this one item, the single strongest and most specifically-Sierra-Leonean claim found.
  - **Suriname (SR):** Hand-woven hammocks, Amerindian woven baskets, Javanese batik textiles -- confirmed via a souvenir-guide source naming hand-woven hammocks, pottery/beadwork/woven baskets from Amerindian groups, and batik fabric from Suriname's Javanese community specifically (a real, distinct heritage strand within Suriname, not a claim about Indonesia). Left out that same source's jewellery and food-pantry items (necklaces/bracelets, cassava bread, ginger candy) to keep the entry to its most craft-distinctive, verifiable items, consistent with how thin most existing 2-3-item entries are elsewhere in the file.
  - **St Lucia (LC):** Coal pot clay cookware, Woven straw baskets -- confirmed via a craft-heritage feature on Choiseul (St Lucia's traditional craft village) naming clay coal pots (a portable cookstove) and straw baskets/hats/mats woven from local sisal and khus khus grass, cross-checked against St Lucia's own tourism-authority page on artisan markets and crafts.

Verified with a script: 157 total keys, all unique, no duplicates; zero raw materials, weapons, or restricted-material (ivory/coral/shell) hints added, per the file's own header rules.

  - **Eswatini (SZ):** Ngwenya Glass (recycled-glass factory/complex, Ngwenya) and Tintsaba (sisal weaving/basketry cooperative, Piggs Peak) -- both confirmed via their own sites (`tintsaba.com`, `thekingdomofeswatini.com`) plus the International Folk Art Market's own profile of Tintsaba.
  - **Grenada (GD):** nutmeg/mace ("Spice Isle," multiple independent tourism/agriculture sources) and cacao/craft chocolate (Grenada's own craft-chocolate industry, confirmed via `spiceislechocolate.com` and an agriculture-news piece on Grenada's "nutmeg renaissance").
  - **Comoros (KM):** ylang-ylang perfume oil (Comoros is repeatedly described as the world's top ylang-ylang producer/"perfume capital," confirmed via `biolandes.com`, `premiumbeautynews.com`, and others) plus vanilla (confirmed via the same "vanilla and ylang-ylang" economics piece).

  - **Lesotho (LS):** Basotho blankets and mokorotlo woven hats -- both confirmed via Wikipedia's own Mokorotlo article plus multiple independent craft/culture sources.
  - **Zambia (ZM):** emerald jewellery (Zambia's Kagem mine is the world's largest emerald mine, confirmed via Gemfields' own site, GIA, and independent mining-press coverage) and Tonga baskets (confirmed via Wikipedia's own Tonga baskets article and an academic/African-IP source).

- **Found a real, new gap:** item 6 previously read "VERIFIED OK" based on confirming the *existing* robots.txt rules (`/api/`, `/dashboard/`, `/admin/`, `/checkout/`, `/orders`) were syntactically correct and pointed at the right sitemap -- it never checked whether that list was *complete* against every real route under `app/`. This run did that fuller pass: listed every top-level directory under `app/`, then read the actual page source of every route that looked plausibly private (`account`, `messages`, `activate`, `auth`, `setup-admin`, `seller`). Confirmed by reading the code, not guessing: `app/account/page.tsx` and `app/account/wishlist/page.tsx` both gate on `useSession`/`status === 'unauthenticated'` and redirect away; `app/messages/page.tsx` does the same (`if (status === 'unauthenticated') router.push('/auth/signin')`); `app/activate/page.tsx` is a tokenized account-activation page (`params.get('token')`, POSTs to `/api/account/activate`) reached only via a private emailed link, the same profile already established as noindex-worthy for `/unsubscribe` (2026-07-12) and `/apply/invited` (2026-07-13); `app/setup-admin/page.tsx` is a public, ungated form that POSTs to `/api/setup-admin` to create an admin account, with no visible frontend secret gate -- regardless of whatever the API route's own auth does, this must never be crawlable/indexable. None of the four export `metadata` (all are `'use client'` pages), so a per-page `robots: noindex` -- the mechanism used for `/unsubscribe`/`/apply/invited` -- wasn't available; robots.txt is the only lever. Verified, not assumed: repo-wide grep confirmed none of the four appear in `app/sitemap.ts` and none are linked from `components/GlobalHeader.tsx` or `components/GlobalFooter.tsx`.
- `app/seller/[sellerId]/page.tsx` was also checked and confirmed to be the *public* seller-profile page (already tracked, unrelated, in backlog item 8), not a private dashboard -- correctly left off this list.
- **Edit made: `app/robots.ts` only.** Added `/account`, `/messages`, `/activate`, `/setup-admin` to the existing `disallow` array, no trailing slash (matches the existing `/orders` convention -- a rule with no trailing slash blocks the bare path, any query string such as `/activate?token=...`, and any subroute in one entry, whereas a trailing-slash rule like `/dashboard/` only blocks subpaths). Added an explanatory comment above the export listing exactly what was found and why, in the same style as the existing rationale comments in `app/sitemap.ts`. No other field, rule, or file touched.

Read `lib/cultureHints.ts`'s own header comment again to confirm its "recruitment copy only -- NEVER rendered as a claim that sellers exist" rule, and wrote the `ItemList`'s `name`/`description` to describe the page (a shopping index), not to claim sellers trade from any specific country -- same discipline `app/origins/[slug]/layout.tsx`'s own description already follows.

### 2026-07-13 19:xx UTC — Gave the brand-new `/origins` and `/origins/[slug]` pages (190 countries) their first metadata; added `/origins` to sitemap.xml; flagged two new judgment-call items

Read `CLAUDE.md`'s new checkpoint entries in full before doing anything else, per standing instructions, rather than trusting the stale backlog line.
- **Found the real, live gap first, backlog update second (not the other way around):** confirmed via `find app/origins -type f` that only two files exist (`app/origins/page.tsx`, `app/origins/[slug]/page.tsx`), both `'use client'`, with **no `layout.tsx` anywhere under `app/origins`** -- meaning both routes had zero metadata of their own and were silently inheriting the generic root `app/layout.tsx` title/description, the exact same gap this agent has fixed for `/founding` and `/shop` in prior runs (2026-07-12). This is a bigger, more valuable, and more time-sensitive fix than working further down the existing backlog (which is otherwise all verified-OK, deferred-pending-data, or flagged judgment calls -- see backlog items 1/2/6-13 above, unchanged) -- a brand-new pair of public routes with zero SEO surface at all outranks incremental polish on already-covered pages.
- Read `lib/cultureHints.ts` in full before using it in any generated metadata, to confirm it is real, already-live editorial content and not something this run would be inventing: it is a curated, product-level "known for" list per country (e.g. Japan: hand-forged kitchen knives, matcha bowls, washi stationery...), already rendered live on both `/origins/[slug]` and `/founding` before this run touched anything, with its own header comment stating the rule ("product-level and culture-first... recruitment copy only -- NEVER rendered as a claim that sellers exist"). Reusing these exact strings in generated `<meta description>` text is consistent with that same rule -- no new fact asserted, no claim that a seller trades from a country that doesn't have one yet.
- **Added `app/origins/layout.tsx`** (static metadata: title, description, `alternates.canonical`, `openGraph`/`twitter` with the existing `/opengraph-image` and `en_GB` locale, mirroring `app/founding/layout.tsx`'s and `app/shop/layout.tsx`'s exact established pattern). Canonical was safe to include here (unlike `/shop`'s deliberate omission, backlog item 2) because the child route gets its own metadata in the same pass -- see next.
- **Added `app/origins/[slug]/layout.tsx`** (dynamic `generateMetadata`, Next 16's async `params: Promise<{slug: string}>` convention, matching the existing convention already used by `app/shop/[productId]/page.tsx`). Resolves the slug via the same `findCountryBySlug()` the page component itself uses; builds title/description from the real country name plus (when present) the first four real `cultureHints` entries; sets a per-country `alternates.canonical`. An unresolvable slug gets a distinct `robots: {index:false}` metadata block instead -- the same treatment already given to other no-real-content states in this codebase (`/search`, `/unsubscribe`, `/apply/invited`).
- **Added `/origins` to `app/sitemap.ts`** (one row, `priority: 0.8`, between `/founding` and `/sell`). Deliberately did **not** add all 190 `/origins/[slug]` URLs in the same pass -- reasoned through in new backlog item 15 rather than decided silently: roughly half the countries have no `cultureHints` entry and no live seller yet, so those specific pages are close to templated boilerplate that could read as thin/near-duplicate content if pushed at scale via a sitemap, versus merely being crawlable (which they already are, via the real internal links on `/origins` itself).
- **New backlog item 14, flagged not decided:** `components/GlobalHeader.tsx`'s "Origins" nav dropdown still links every country to `/founding`, not `/origins/[slug]` -- confirmed via `CLAUDE.md`'s own "checkpoint (continued 15)" entry that this was a deliberate choice by the building session ("William only asked to build the missing pages, not rewire navigation... that's a separate decision to offer, not make unprompted"), so this is a live internal-linking gap this agent should flag, not silently fix, per the same standing-directive-4 reasoning that session itself already applied.
- **Backlog item 4 rewritten** (marked `[SUPERSEDED]` rather than deleted, so the reasoning trail stays intact) to reflect that the pages now exist and have metadata, while being explicit that William's original, deeper "15+ researched cultural items per country, cited sources" vision is still a distinct, unstarted project -- `cultureHints` is real but much shallower than that.

- **Found and fixed one genuinely new, safe, additive gap:** `openGraph.locale` was missing from every `openGraph` object in the codebase (confirmed via `grep -rln openGraph app --include="*.tsx"` -- 21 files, `app/layout.tsx` included). Per the same `vercel/next.js#50353` behavior already documented in this log (an explicit `openGraph` object on a route replaces the *whole* object, so a value only added to the root layout would not propagate to any of the 20 routes that define their own `openGraph`), fixing this correctly meant touching all 21 files, the same scope as the 2026-07-13 15:xx UTC `og:image` fix. Value used: `'en_GB'`, verified against two independent, already-live sources rather than guessed -- `lib/currency.ts`'s `getCurrency()` falls back to `'GBP'` as the site's real base/default currency (both in its no-`navigator` branch and its final `return`), and `app/api/briefing/route.ts` already formats those same GBP figures using the `'en-GB'` `Intl.NumberFormat` locale elsewhere in this codebase. `en_GB` is consistent with both facts, not a third, disconnected guess. Inserted directly after each file's existing `siteName: 'Velor',` line via a small Python script (to guarantee identical placement and formatting across all 21 files rather than 21 manual edits), with a full-rationale comment in `app/layout.tsx` and a short pointer-back comment in the other 20 files, matching the established comment-scoping pattern from the `og:image` fix. No title, description, image, canonical, or robots field touched anywhere -- purely additive, one new field per file.

The 2026-07-13 11:xx UTC run had explicitly considered and ruled out `Organization.address`, stating "no postal address ... is published anywhere on the live site or confirmed in `CLAUDE.md` to verify against." That check was scoped to `CLAUDE.md` and existing JSON-LD/metadata fields -- this run instead read live page body copy directly (`app/legal/terms/page.tsx`, `app/contact/page.tsx`, `app/about/page.tsx`) and found a real, live, verified registered-office address stated identically in all three: "Velor Commerce Ltd (company no. 17268133), registered office 49 Station Road, Polegate, East Sussex, BN26 6EA." Confirmed via `grep -rn "17268133"` that all three occurrences match word-for-word (modulo "company number"/"company no." phrasing) -- three independent, consistent live citations, a stronger internal-verification bar than this file's own `legalName` field was added against (a single `CLAUDE.md` line).
- Attempted a fourth, independent verification via a live Companies House lookup for company number 17268133 (`WebSearch` first, which surfaced several similarly-named but non-matching UK companies -- VELOR LIMITED, VELORA COMMERCE LTD, etc., none with that exact number -- then a direct `WebFetch` of the Companies House company page for that number) to positively confirm the registration before writing it into structured data, since JSON-LD is a stronger, more machine-trusted claim than internal body copy alone. The `WebFetch` was blocked by `PROVENANCE_REQUIRED` (see sandbox note above) and could not be completed this run. Logging this honestly rather than either fabricating a "confirmed via Companies House" claim or silently skipping the caveat: the three-live-page internal consistency check was completed and passed; the external Companies House check was attempted and blocked, not completed.
- Verified the schema.org shape before writing it: `WebSearch` for schema.org's `PostalAddress` type and `address` property returned the expected canonical property names (`streetAddress`, `addressLocality`, `addressRegion`, `postalCode`, `addressCountry`) directly in the result titles from `schema.org/PostalAddress`, `schema.org/address`, `schema.org/streetAddress`, and `schema.org/addressLocality` -- a well-established, standard schema.org type, consistent with every implementation guide surfaced.
- Added `address: { '@type': 'PostalAddress', streetAddress: '49 Station Road', addressLocality: 'Polegate', addressRegion: 'East Sussex', postalCode: 'BN26 6EA', addressCountry: 'GB' }` to the existing `Organization` node in `app/layout.tsx`, directly above `sameAs`. An inline code comment records the full sourcing (the three live pages, the ruled-out-then-reversed history, and the blocked Companies House check) so a future run does not have to re-derive any of this or mistake it for a fresh unverified claim.
- No copy, titles, descriptions, canonicals, robots directives, or other existing JSON-LD field values were changed anywhere -- purely additive, one new field on the existing `Organization` object in one file.

### 2026-07-13 15:xx UTC — Found and fixed a real, site-wide gap: 20 routes had no og:image/twitter:image at all

Full technical audit was not re-run -- the last full audit was 2026-07-12 18:12 UTC, still under 24h old per this log's own rule (current time ~15:08 UTC, ~21h since that audit). Worked the backlog top-to-bottom first, same as every recent run: cloned the live repo fresh, diffed both `CLAUDE.md` and this log against `raw.githubusercontent.com` copies fetched at session start -- byte-identical this run, no staleness. Repo tip at clone time: `87f66f3` (three small non-SEO commits landed mid-run, Payoneer coming-soon copy -- fast-forward-merged cleanly before pushing, see Push section). Re-read `CLAUDE.md` in full -- no product or seller has been added, catalogue still confirmed at zero, so items 1/2/4/8/10 remain genuinely blocked for the same reasons already logged. Items 6/7 remain verified-OK. Items 9 (stray file), 11 (`/apply/verified` indexing), and 12 (favicon) remain flagged judgment calls for William, not acted on this run either.

- **Sandbox note this run:** `api.github.com` Contents API returned the same `403 {"message":"GitHub access to this repository is not enabled for this session. Use add_repo to request access."}` seen in every prior run. `raw.githubusercontent.com` and `git clone`/`git fetch` against the pre-wired local proxy remote both worked immediately (this run's proxy port was `41729`, same as recent prior runs). `WebFetch` against the live site itself (`https://velorcommerce.store/apply`) was attempted, to directly inspect rendered `<head>` tags rather than reason from source alone, but returned `PROVENANCE_REQUIRED` -- an approval prompt with nobody there to answer it, since this is an unattended scheduled run. Fell back to verifying the underlying mechanism against Next.js's own documentation and a real Next.js GitHub discussion instead (see below) -- both are external documentation sources, not the live site, so no fabricated claim about the site itself was needed to reach a confident conclusion.
- Considered backlog item 5's "still outstanding" remaining piece first (weaving origin/heritage body copy into `/apply`, `/founding`, `/about`). Read all three pages' live body copy directly and found this is, in substance, already done by other (non-SEO-agent) sessions since that backlog line was last written: `/apply` and `/founding` both already lead with "Be the first from your country" plus the founding-perk mechanic in the hero and perk box; `/about` already opens "Buy from the source. Sell to the world." and closes with "Make something your country is known for?". Not logged as a backlog change this run (a full backlog rewrite is audit work, not backlog work, and the next full audit -- not due for ~3 more hours -- is the right place to reconcile this line properly); flagging here so the next audit can update or retire that line instead of re-deriving this from scratch.
- Looked for a new, safe, additive gap outside the numbered backlog instead, the same way recent runs found `llms.txt` and the various JSON-LD fields. Checked social-share readiness (part of this agent's standing AI-visibility/answer-engine brief, and directly relevant to `CLAUDE.md`'s own extensive log of Facebook-group outreach links). Found: `app/opengraph-image.tsx` exists at the repo root (a real, working `next/og` `ImageResponse` generator, confirmed by reading it), but a repo-wide check of every route defining its own `openGraph`/`twitter` metadata object (21 files, via `grep -rln openGraph app`) found only one, `app/sell/layout.tsx`, actually lists an `images` field pointing at it. The other 20 -- including the **root `app/layout.tsx` itself**, which sits in the very same folder as the image generator -- define an `openGraph`/`twitter` object with no `images` field at all.
- Verified, rather than assumed, that this is a real gap and not just a stylistic inconsistency: fetched Next.js's own docs (`nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/metadata-and-og-images`, version 16.2.10, confirmed current) which state the more-specific-wins rule for the file convention itself, then searched further and fetched a live Next.js GitHub discussion (`vercel/next.js#50353`) where a Next.js collaborator confirms in plain terms: "overwriting *any* part of `openGraph` in the metadata object replaces all of `openGraph`" -- meaning any route (or the root itself) that defines its own `openGraph` object without an explicit `images` field gets **no** og:image at all, not a silent fallback to the nearest file-convention image up the tree. The discussion's own listed workaround is exactly the fix already proven correct in this codebase: list the image explicitly in each route's `openGraph`/`twitter` object, which is precisely what `app/sell/layout.tsx` already does.
- Fixed all 20 remaining routes, using `app/sell/layout.tsx`'s exact existing pattern (same generated image URL, same 1200x630 dimensions, same alt text, referenced not retyped, so it can never drift): root `app/layout.tsx`, `/marketplace`, `/legal/seller-rules`, `/legal/seller-agreement`, `/legal/terms`, `/legal/privacy`, `/search`, `/apply/verified`, `/apply`, `/founding`, `/returns`, `/seller-agreement`, `/contact`, `/cookies`, `/track`, `/shop`, `/help`, `/live`, `/live/[room]`, `/about`. This asserts no new fact and changes no title, description, canonical, robots directive, or JSON-LD field anywhere -- purely additive, one `images` array added to an `openGraph` object and one to a `twitter` object per file, all pointing at the same already-live, already-working generated image.
- One deliberate judgment call, flagged inline in the diff itself: `/shop`'s new fallback image will be inherited by `/shop/[productId]` (which has no metadata of its own -- see backlog item 2), unlike its `alternates.canonical`, which is deliberately withheld for exactly that inheritance reason. Judged safe to leave in place: a generic brand image asserts no false per-product claim the way a shared canonical URL would (a canonical says "this URL is really that other URL"; a fallback image just says "here is a picture to show," true for every route). Not treated as a new backlog item, since it's a one-line, already-resolved judgment call, not an open question.

**Verification before pushing:** ran `npm install` (fresh in this sandbox instance -- 171 packages, no errors) and `npx tsc --noEmit` against the full repo. Same pre-existing-error pattern as every prior run: exactly 159 `error TS` lines. Grepped the `tsc` output specifically for all 20 touched files (by path) -- zero matches, confirming none of them introduced a new error. `git diff --stat` before committing showed exactly 20 files changed, 132 insertions, 0 deletions -- matching the intended scope exactly (one `images` field added to each of two objects per file, nothing else touched).

**Push:** confirmed the proxy remote (`http://127.0.0.1:41729/git/BILSY144/velor-marketplace.git`) still returns a plain `403` on `git push`, tested directly this run. Before switching to the PAT workaround, ran `git fetch origin main` and found `origin/main` had moved 3 commits ahead of the clone (Payoneer "coming soon" copy changes to `CLAUDE.md`, `app/sell/page.tsx`, `components/GlobalFooter.tsx` -- none overlapping this run's 20 files) -- fast-forward-merged (`git merge origin/main --ff-only`) cleanly before committing, so this run's commit sits cleanly on top rather than diverging. Used the documented workaround: `git remote set-url origin "https://x-access-token:<PAT>@github.com/BILSY144/velor-marketplace.git"`, then pushed -- `4be09e9..e476d6c main -> main` with no errors. **Confirmed pushed:** a follow-up `git fetch` + `git rev-list --count` both directions (`HEAD..origin/main` and `origin/main..HEAD`) returned `0` and `0`, confirming local and remote `main` match exactly at commit `e476d6c` ("SEO: add fallback og:image/twitter:image to 20 routes' openGraph/twitter metadata"). Reset the remote URL back to the proxy remote (without the PAT embedded) immediately after confirming the push, before this log update. This log update is a separate, second commit pushed immediately after, for the same reason as every prior run -- it needed to reference the code commit's real SHA.

Google's own Organization structured-data guidance does not require both `email` and `contactPoint.email` together, but having both is valid per schema.org and gives search engines and AI-answer-engine crawlers an unambiguous, top-level contact fact for the entity even if they only read one level deep.

Used the freshly cloned repo's actual file contents as the source of truth for this run rather than the `raw.githubusercontent.com` fetch, and am flagging this discrepancy here in case a future run relies on `raw.githubusercontent.com` alone and gets a stale picture of the backlog/log.

With the `Organization`/`WebSite` objects' individual recommended properties now fairly saturated, checked the structural side instead: neither `@graph` entry had an `@id`, meaning `WebSite` and `Organization` render as two disconnected, anonymous nodes rather than a linked entity graph -- a well-documented Google/schema.org pattern (stable `#organization`/`#website` fragment identifiers, `WebSite.publisher` referencing `Organization` by `@id`) that several major sites' own JSON-LD implementations use.
- Added `'@id': 'https://velorcommerce.store/#organization'` to the `Organization` node, `'@id': 'https://velorcommerce.store/#website'` to the `WebSite` node, and `publisher: { '@id': 'https://velorcommerce.store/#organization' }` on `WebSite` referencing the `Organization` node already defined above it in the same `@graph` array. This asserts no new fact -- it is a purely structural change that ties two already-live, already-verified objects together instead of leaving them as duplicate unlinked entities. Both `@id` values were sanity-checked against schema.org's own documented `@id`-as-fragment-URL convention before writing them, not invented ad hoc.
- No copy, titles, descriptions, canonicals, robots directives, or existing JSON-LD field values were changed anywhere -- purely additive, three new fields (two `@id`, one `publisher`) in the same one file every prior JSON-LD run has touched.

### 2026-07-13 12:xx UTC — Added `viewport` export (themeColor) to root layout

Full technical audit was not re-run -- the last full audit was 2026-07-12 18:12 UTC, still under 24h old per this log's own rule (current time ~12:18 UTC, ~18h since that audit). Worked the backlog top-to-bottom first, same as every recent run: re-checked `CLAUDE.md` for any new commits or catalogue change since the 11:xx UTC run -- `git log --oneline -15` showed no commits since this log's own last entry, and `CLAUDE.md`'s newest sections (Facebook outreach parts 1-3, the Step 6 organic-channel addendum) still contain no mention of any product or seller being added, so items 1/2/4/8/10 remain genuinely blocked for the same reasons already logged. Re-checked item 1's specific blocker directly rather than trusting the backlog text alone: `grep -rn breadcrumb app/shop` still zero matches. Items 6/7 remain verified-OK. Items 9 (stray file), 11 (`/apply/verified` indexing), and 12 (favicon) remain flagged judgment calls for William, not acted on this run either.

- Cloned the live repo fresh against the pre-wired proxy remote and diffed both `CLAUDE.md` and this log against the `raw.githubusercontent.com` copies fetched at session start -- byte-identical, no staleness this run. Repo tip at start of this run: `5dd4635`.
- **Sandbox note this run:** `api.github.com` Contents API (tried first, per this agent's standing instructions) returned `403 {"message":"GitHub access to this repository is not enabled for this session. Use add_repo to request access."}`, same restriction every prior run has hit -- confirmed this session has no `add_repo`-equivalent tool available to it. `raw.githubusercontent.com` and `git clone`/`git fetch` against the pre-wired local proxy remote both worked immediately, consistent with every prior run. Also confirmed this run that a general internet connectivity test (`curl https://www.google.com`) returned no connection (exit/timeout), while `github.com`/`raw.githubusercontent.com`/`api.github.com` all returned real HTTP responses -- consistent with this log's existing note that only GitHub-related hosts are reachable from this sandbox.
- Went looking for a genuinely new, safe, additive gap outside the numbered backlog, the same way recent runs found `sameAs`, `contactPoint`, `inLanguage`, `legalName`, `description`, and `areaServed`. This run looked outside the JSON-LD `Organization`/`WebSite` objects (which are now fairly saturated against schema.org's recommended-property list) and instead checked Next.js's separate `viewport` metadata API repo-wide: `grep -rn themeColor app` found exactly one hit, `app/pulse/layout.tsx` (a private, `noindex` dashboard), which sets `export const viewport: Viewport = { width: 'device-width', initialScale: 1, themeColor: '#0d0d0d' }`. The root `app/layout.tsx` -- the file every public page either uses directly or inherits from -- had no `viewport` export at all, meaning every real page falls back to Next.js's bare default (device-width + initialScale only, no theme-color), unlike the one private page that already does this correctly.
- Verified the correct value before writing it, rather than copying `/pulse`'s `#0d0d0d` on the assumption it was the same color: read `app/globals.css` directly and found the real default (unqualified `:root` block, i.e. what actually renders before any theme toggle) sets `--bg: #0d0d0f` -- one hex digit different from `/pulse`'s value, so copying it would have been a small but avoidable inaccuracy. Also checked how the light/dark toggle itself works, since `theme-color` supports a `media`-array split by `prefers-color-scheme`: found `html[data-theme='light']` is the override selector, applied via a JS-driven `data-theme` attribute (`components/GlobalHeader.tsx`, `components/ThemePreview.tsx`), not a `prefers-color-scheme` CSS media query -- so a `light`/`dark` media-query split on `theme-color` would not reliably match what a user has actually toggled to, and a single static value matching the real, unqualified default (dark, `#0d0d0f`) is the accurate choice, same reasoning already applied by whoever set `/pulse`'s value the same way.
- Added `export const viewport: Viewport = { width: 'device-width', initialScale: 1, themeColor: '#0d0d0f' }` to `app/layout.tsx`, with an inline comment citing both source facts (`/pulse`'s existing pattern, `globals.css`'s real `--bg` value) and the light/dark reasoning above, so a future run doesn't have to re-derive it. This affects mobile browser chrome (address bar) color and PWA/add-to-home-screen presentation -- a real, verifiable technical-SEO/mobile-UX gap, not content.

Worked the backlog top-to-bottom first: items 1/2/8 still blocked (catalogue still confirmed at zero via `CLAUDE.md`'s newest entries), items 4 and 9/10 remain deliberate deferrals/judgment calls for William. Picked backlog item 5 (competitor/keyword-gap, on-page copy) as the highest-priority item that was both unblocked and safe.

- Read every metadata-exporting route added or touched by prior runs (`/apply`, `/sell`, `/founding`, `/shop`, `/help`, `/about`) and confirmed each already carries cultural-heritage-forward title/description copy referencing "authentic cultural and heritage goods," "190 countries," and "its maker and its country of origin." Then checked the one file every one of those routes' own metadata is designed to override: `app/layout.tsx`, the root layout the homepage and any future metadata-less route inherits from. It still had the original pre-positioning copy: title `Velor - Global Marketplace`, description `Discover unique products from sellers around the world.` -- generic, un-differentiated from Etsy/eBay/Amazon Handmade, and the one page on the entire site not reflecting the heritage/origin positioning, despite the homepage being the single highest-value page for organic search.
- Verified before writing new copy, not assumed: grepped the codebase for the exact phrases used (`authentic cultural and heritage goods` in `app/apply/layout.tsx`; `independent sellers ... from 190 countries` and `its maker and its country of origin` in `app/sell/layout.tsx`; `190 countries` also appears in `app/founding/layout.tsx`, `app/page.tsx`'s own swipe-hint copy, and `app/live/page.tsx`) and counted `WORLD_COUNTRIES` entries (191 codes, but every existing page consistently uses the site's own established "190 countries" figure, so that figure was reused rather than the raw array length, to stay consistent with the rest of the site rather than introduce a second, conflicting number).
- New copy, built only from that verified, already-live language (nothing invented): title `Velor — Global Marketplace for Culture & Heritage` (49 characters, within the ~50-60 char guideline); description `Shop live or browse authentic cultural and heritage goods from independent sellers in 190 countries — each listing carries its maker and origin.` (144 characters, within the ~150-160 char guideline, not truncation-risk-short either). Applied to `title`, `description`, `openGraph.title`/`openGraph.description`, and `twitter.title`/`twitter.description` via shared `const title`/`const description` variables, matching the pattern every other metadata-exporting layout in this codebase already uses.
- Deliberately did NOT add a canonical to `app/layout.tsx` -- per backlog item 3's standing reasoning, a homepage canonical on the shared root layout would silently apply to every route in the app that has no metadata export of its own, which is not yet a safe, fully-audited state. Deliberately did NOT touch `metadataBase`, the JSON-LD `Organization`/`WebSite` schema block, or any other file -- purely the four title/description string pairs.

**Verification before pushing:** ran `npm install` (fresh in this sandbox instance -- 438 packages, no errors) and `npx tsc --noEmit` against the full repo. Same pre-existing-error pattern as every prior run: exactly 159 `error TS` lines; grepped the output specifically for `app/layout.tsx` -- zero matches, confirming no new type error from this change (still could not run `next build` here, same Prisma-binary-403 sandbox limitation noted in every prior run). `git diff --stat` before committing showed exactly 1 file changed, 25 insertions, 6 deletions -- matching the intended scope exactly.

**Push:** the auto-configured proxy remote (`http://127.0.0.1:41729/git/BILSY144/velor-marketplace.git`) returned a plain `403` on `git push`, same as every prior run. Ran `git fetch origin main` first and found `origin/main` had not moved since the clone (`0` commits either direction via `git rev-list --count`) -- no rebase needed. Used the documented workaround: `git remote set-url origin "https://x-access-token:<PAT>@github.com/BILSY144/velor-marketplace.git"`, then pushed -- `0d9a17d..110daa7 main -> main` with no errors. **Confirmed pushed:** a follow-up `git fetch` + `git rev-list --count` both directions (`HEAD..origin/main` and `origin/main..HEAD`) returned `0` and `0`, confirming local and remote `main` match exactly at commit `110daa7` ("SEO: rewrite homepage title/description to match site's cultural-heritage positioning"). Reset the remote URL back to the proxy remote (without the PAT embedded) immediately after confirming the push, before this log update. This log update is a separate, second commit pushed immediately after, for the same reason as every prior run -- it needed to reference the code commit's real SHA.

**Vercel deployment:** not independently checked this run, per this log's own standing rule (Vercel auto-notifies William directly, by web and email, on any deployment failure -- no action needed from this agent). Also attempted the session's `WebFetch` tool against `https://velorcommerce.store` directly, as an extra check beyond the standing rule -- it returned a `PROVENANCE_REQUIRED` error requiring a live user to approve the fetch, same result as every prior run's attempt, which is not available in this unattended scheduled run. Logging that attempt and its result honestly rather than omitting it or claiming the live homepage title was checked.

### 2026-07-13 05:xx UTC — Added `sameAs` (Facebook Page) to Organization JSON-LD schema; ruled out a false-positive OG-image gap

Full technical audit was not re-run -- the last full audit was 2026-07-12 18:12 UTC, under 24h old per this log's own rule. Cloned the live repo fresh against the pre-wired proxy remote and diffed `CLAUDE.md`/`SEO_LOG.md` against the `raw.githubusercontent.com` copies fetched at session start -- byte-identical, no staleness this run. Repo tip at start of this run: `10ff4c7`.

- Worked the backlog top-to-bottom first, same as the 2026-07-13 04:xx UTC run: re-confirmed items 1/2/8 are still blocked (`grep -rn "breadcrumb" app/shop` -- zero matches; `CLAUDE.md`'s newest 2026-07-13 SESSION UPDATE entries, about Facebook outreach and a Step 6 organic-channel plan, contain no mention of any product or seller being added -- the 2026-07-08 catalogue purge to zero is still the last confirmed state). Item 4 (`/origins`) and item 5 (copy-weaving) remain deliberately not started for the same reasons as every prior run. Item 9 (stray file) remains a deletion judgment call for William, not this agent. Confirmed no new public route has appeared without metadata: repo-wide `find app -name page.tsx -o -name layout.tsx` (87 files) and a metadata-export grep (24 files, up from the 22 recorded in backlog item 3 -- the increase is `/apply/invited`, `/live`, `/live/[room]` landing since that count was written, all already accounted for in the backlog text) turned up nothing new; `app/marketplace`, `app/seller`, `app/shop` dynamic-route structure matches exactly what backlog items 2 and 8 already describe.
- While re-checking the 2026-07-13 02:xx UTC run's `openGraph`/`twitter` additions, noticed only `app/sell/layout.tsx` sets an explicit `openGraph.images` field (pointing at `/opengraph-image`); every other metadata-exporting route does not. Investigated before logging this as a gap, per the "verify, don't assume" step: `app/opengraph-image.tsx` exists at the `app/` root and uses the Next.js App Router file-based metadata convention (`export const alt`, `export const size`, `export const contentType`, default-exported `Image()` using `next/og`'s `ImageResponse`) -- per Next.js's own documented behavior, a root-level `opengraph-image.tsx` is automatically resolved into the `openGraph.images` field for every route that does not itself override it, with no manual `images` array needed. So this was a false positive, not a real gap: every route already gets a real, correct OG image (the dark Velor "GLOBAL MARKETPLACE" card, confirmed by reading the file's actual render output) via the file convention; `/sell`'s manual `images` field is a harmless, redundant duplicate of what it would already inherit, not a fix for a missing one. No code change made for this -- correctly ruling something out is itself the useful outcome here, logged so a future run doesn't re-investigate the same non-issue.
- Found a genuine, real, additive gap instead: the `Organization` object in `app/layout.tsx`'s JSON-LD `@graph` (added 2026-07-12 19:08 UTC) has no `sameAs` array. `sameAs` is the standard schema.org mechanism for telling Google/AI answer engines which social profiles are the same real-world entity as the site -- directly relevant to this log's own "AI-visibility/answer-engine readiness" audit criterion, and to the fact that William's primary current growth channel is exactly the kind of social presence `sameAs` is meant to surface (per `CLAUDE.md`'s Facebook Group outreach sections, the daily `outreach-auto` Facebook task, and the newly-added Step 6 organic-channel plan).
- Checked what social profiles are real and confirmed before adding anything, per LAW #1 -- did not invent a URL: `CLAUDE.md`'s 2026-07-12 "Facebook Group Outreach" session update states directly "Posting is done from the 'VELOR' Facebook Page identity (facebook.com/Velorcommerce), not a personal profile," and logs multiple real, live posts made from that identity the same day. This is the project's own operational record, not a guess -- treated as verified the same way prior runs have treated other `CLAUDE.md`-sourced facts (e.g. the logo file path, the `APPLICATION_SLA_HOURS` constant) as ground truth without a live re-fetch, since this sandbox has no general outbound network access to independently browse facebook.com and confirm it live (confirmed again this run: `curl` to `facebook.com` and `google.com` both returned no connection, exit 56, same restriction documented in this log's Rules section -- only `github.com`/`raw.githubusercontent.com` are reachable). Added exactly one entry: `sameAs: ['https://www.facebook.com/Velorcommerce']`.
- Deliberately did NOT add Instagram, Pinterest, or X/Twitter to `sameAs`: `CLAUDE.md` explicitly states Instagram is "blocked, unresolved" (no login/connection exists), that a Pinterest business account is "unconfirmed either way" whether one exists yet, and Step 6's X/Twitter plan describes replying to *other* accounts, not operating a confirmed Velor-owned X profile. Adding any of these would have meant fabricating or guessing a URL for an account that isn't confirmed to exist -- not done, per LAW #1. Revisit `sameAs` once any of these becomes a real, confirmed, live account (tracked in `CLAUDE.md`'s own outreach sections, not duplicated into this backlog).
- No copy, titles, descriptions, or existing schema fields were changed -- purely additive, one new field on the existing `Organization` object. `git diff --stat` before committing showed exactly 1 file changed, 1 insertion.
- Backlog left unchanged: nothing was newly unblocked, and this run's fix was a new finding outside the numbered list, not a completion of an existing item.

**Verification before pushing:** ran `npm install` (fresh in this sandbox instance -- 171 packages, no errors) and `npx tsc --noEmit` against the full repo. Same pre-existing-error pattern as every prior run: exactly 159 `error TS` lines, all pre-existing and unrelated to this change (same Prisma-binary-403 sandbox limitation noted in every prior run, so `next build` still could not be run here). Grepped the `tsc` output specifically for `app/layout` -- zero matches. `git status` before committing showed only `app/layout.tsx` modified plus the untracked `package-lock.json` byproduct of this run's fresh install (left uncommitted, same as every prior run).

**Push:** the auto-configured proxy remote (`http://127.0.0.1:41729/git/BILSY144/velor-marketplace.git`) returned a plain `403` on `git push`, same as every prior run. Before switching to the PAT workaround, ran `git fetch origin main` and found `origin/main` had not moved since the clone (`0` commits either direction via `git rev-list --count`) -- no rebase needed. Used the documented workaround: `git remote set-url origin "https://x-access-token:<PAT>@github.com/BILSY144/velor-marketplace.git"`, then pushed -- `10ff4c7..7f61a37 main -> main` with no errors. **Confirmed pushed:** a follow-up `git fetch` + `git rev-list --count` both directions (`HEAD..origin/main` and `origin/main..HEAD`) returned `0` and `0`, confirming local and remote `main` match exactly at commit `7f61a37` ("SEO: add sameAs (Facebook Page) to Organization JSON-LD schema"). Initial grep for `<img` lines without `alt=` on the same line flagged 6 files, but reading each in full context showed this was a false positive of the grep itself (JSX spreads `<img` attributes across multiple lines): all 6 do have `alt` -- either a real descriptive value (`alt={product.title}`, `alt={seller.country}`, `alt={item.product.name}`, `alt={t.name}`, `alt={name}`) or a deliberate `alt=""` on a genuinely decorative/redundant image (the `CountryOriginStrip.tsx` flag icon, whose parent `<button>` already carries `aria-label={\`Shop products from ${c.name}\`}` and `title={c.name}`, so an empty alt correctly avoids a screen reader double-announcement; and the `ProductDetail.tsx` gallery thumbnails, which sit beside a main image already carrying `alt={product.title}`). No defect found -- backlog item 7 ("verified OK, no action") still holds.
- Ran a repo-wide internal-link audit that hadn't been done as its own pass before: extracted every static `href="/..."` string across `app/**/*.tsx` and `components/**/*.tsx` (34 unique paths, via a small Python script rather than a hand grep, to correctly handle both `href="..."` and `href={...}` forms) and checked each against the App Router's real file structure (`app/<path>/page.tsx` or `route.ts`). - Item 9 (stray `app/feat: ...` file) was re-confirmed unchanged and was, again, deliberately not deleted -- still a judgment call for William or a session with clearer deletion authority, not this agent acting unilaterally.
- Conclusion: no backlog item was newly unblocked, and the extra checks run this cycle (alt text, internal links) found nothing new to fix. Backlog left unchanged. No code was pushed this run -- there was nothing safe and additive to push. This log update itself is the only change.

It matched one file: `app/feat: add Google Fonts and CSS variables to layout` -- not `GlobalHeader.tsx` or any real route. Investigated this match rather than ignoring it: it is a stray, un-routed file inside `app/` (commit `f1df1a3`, 2026-06-30) whose name is an old commit message, evidently created by a filename mistake in an earlier session, predating this SEO agent. Confirmed it cannot affect the live build -- its name matches no Next.js App Router special filename, so it is never built or served as a route (`npx tsc --noEmit` was grepped for it: zero references), and the real `app/layout.tsx` exists separately and is current (has the JSON-LD schema and current title/description; this stray file has neither, and still references the dead `Navigation.tsx`). Logged as new backlog item 11 rather than deleted -- this run stayed inside the additive/safe-fix lane per the standing rule; deleting files (even confirmed-dead ones) is a judgment call for William or a session with clearer authority, not something this agent unilaterally decided to do. Backlog item 4 itself is therefore still open and unchanged.
- While investigating, found a second, unrelated, more actionable gap: repo-wide check of every file exporting Next.js `metadata` (23 files) showed 11 of them had `title`/`description`/`alternates.canonical` but no `openGraph`/`twitter` block, unlike `/apply`, `/founding`, `/help`, `/sell`, `/contact`, and `/live`, which already carry both (added in prior runs). Missing on: `/about`, `/apply/verified`, `/cookies`, `/legal/privacy`, `/legal/seller-agreement`, `/legal/seller-rules`, `/legal/terms`, `/marketplace`, `/returns`, `/seller-agreement`, `/track`. This matters in particular because William's own seller-recruitment channel right now is social (Facebook groups, per the SELLER ACQUISITION PLAN Step 3 in `CLAUDE.md`) -- a link shared into a group without `openGraph` tags renders as a bare URL instead of a titled preview card, which is exactly the kind of thing that reads as spammy and gets a post buried, the same failure mode already diagnosed in that section for a different reason.
- Fixed all 11: added `openGraph: { title, description, url, siteName: 'Velor', type: 'website' }` and `twitter: { card: 'summary_large_image', title, description }` to each file's existing `metadata` object, reusing that file's own title/description verbatim -- nothing paraphrased or invented, per LAW #1. Followed the exact structure already live in `app/apply/layout.tsx`. Two of the eleven (`/cookies`, `/returns`, `/track`) have no `description` field at all in the source `metadata` (title + canonical only) -- did not invent one to fill the `openGraph`/`twitter` blocks; their new blocks carry `title` only, matching what the page itself declares.
- Noticed in passing, but deliberately did NOT act on: three of the eleven (`/cookies`, `/returns`, `/track`) title their page "... - Velor Commerce" while every other metadata-exporting route says "Velor Marketplace" or "Velor". Did not touch this. `CLAUDE.md` line 28 lists the Stripe account behind this marketplace itself as registered to "VELOR COMMERCE LTD" -- so "Velor Commerce" may be the correct legal-entity name for transactional/legal-adjacent pages, not a mix-up with the separate velorcommerce.co.uk business. Genuinely unconfirmed which reading is right; flagging here rather than guessing, per LAW #1, rather than either leaving it silently unmentioned or "fixing" something that might not be broken.
- No copy, titles, descriptions, or canonicals were changed on any of the 11 files -- purely additive, one new `openGraph` object and one new `twitter` object per file.

**Verification before pushing:** ran `npm install` (fresh in this sandbox instance -- 171 packages, no errors) and `npx tsc --noEmit` against the full repo. Grepped the `tsc` output specifically for all 11 edited file paths -- zero matches. `git diff --stat` before committing showed exactly the 11 intended files, 148 insertions / 3 deletions (the 3 deletions are the one-line `metadata` objects in `/cookies`, `/returns`, `/track` reformatted from single-line to multi-line to hold the new blocks -- re-read each diff directly to confirm no existing field's value changed, only formatting plus new fields added).

This is exactly the same inheritance risk already flagged and avoided for `/shop`/`/shop/[productId]` in backlog items 2 and 3 -- handled correctly from the start this time rather than left as a follow-up.

Full technical audit was not re-run (last one was 18:12 UTC today, well under 24h old per this log's own rule). - Confirmed all four `app/legal/*` pages (`seller-agreement`, `terms`, `privacy`, `seller-rules`) are live, linked pages -- not dead code -- via repo-wide grep: `components/GlobalFooter.tsx` links `/legal/seller-agreement`, `/legal/privacy`, `/legal/terms` directly; `/legal/seller-rules` is linked from `app/apply/page.tsx`, `app/apply/verified/page.tsx`, `app/dashboard/products/page.tsx`, `app/dashboard/terms/page.tsx`, and `app/seller-agreement/page.tsx`'s own body copy.
- Added `export const metadata` (title, description, `alternates.canonical`) directly to all four `app/legal/*/page.tsx` files -- they are plain server components (no `'use client'`), so no layout wrapper was needed, unlike the client-component routes handled in prior runs. Every description was written from that page's own real section headings (extracted via grep of each page's `sections` array, e.g. terms' 12 numbered sections, privacy's 10, seller-rules' 13, seller-agreement's 11) -- nothing invented, per LAW #1.
- Noticed a pre-existing naming collision worth flagging: there are two separate, both-live "Seller Agreement" routes -- the top-level `app/seller-agreement/page.tsx` (already had metadata from a prior run, title "Seller Agreement — Velor Marketplace") and `app/legal/seller-agreement/page.tsx` (this run's new metadata). Gave the `/legal/` one a distinct title ("Seller Agreement — Legal Terms — Velor Marketplace") specifically to avoid shipping two pages with an identical `<title>`, which would itself be a minor SEO defect. Did not attempt to merge, redirect, or otherwise resolve the underlying duplication between the two routes -- that is a product/content decision, not something this agent should change unilaterally; flagging it here for William's awareness rather than acting on it.
- `/contact` (`'use client'`) got a new `app/contact/layout.tsx` server-component wrapper, following the exact pattern already live for `/help` and `/apply` -- title/description/canonical/OpenGraph/Twitter, copy drawn from the page's own live hero ("Talk to Velor.") and reply-time line.
- `/search` (`'use client'`, query-param driven via `?q=`) got a new `app/search/layout.tsx` wrapper too, but deliberately set to `robots: { index: false, follow: true }` instead of given a canonical. Reasoning: this is an internal site-search results page -- indexing every `?q=` variation as a distinct URL is thin/duplicate content by definition, and noindexing internal search is standard, uncontroversial technical SEO practice, not a judgment call requiring further research. `follow: true` keeps the outbound links to real product/category pages from search results crawlable. This is the same underlying category of reasoning as the `/shop` canonical omission from a prior run (avoid falsely telling search engines something), just applied via `noindex` instead of via canonical omission since search results carry no reusable canonical URL to point to.
- No copy, titles, or descriptions were changed on any of the six pages themselves -- purely additive, new metadata objects and two new thin layout files only.

### 2026-07-12 21:17 UTC — Fixed backlog item 3 (mostly): self-referencing canonical tags on 11 routes

Full technical audit was not re-run (last one was 18:12 UTC today, well under 24h old per this log's own rule). Worked the backlog: item 3, "No canonical tags anywhere in the codebase."

- Repo-wide grep before starting found 14 files exporting Next.js `metadata` (more than the 7 "key pages" the standing audit brief names): `app/layout.tsx` (root), `app/about/page.tsx`, `app/apply/layout.tsx`, `app/apply/verified/page.tsx`, `app/cookies/page.tsx`, `app/founding/layout.tsx`, `app/help/layout.tsx`, `app/marketplace/page.tsx`, `app/pulse/layout.tsx`, `app/returns/page.tsx`, `app/sell/layout.tsx`, `app/seller-agreement/page.tsx`, `app/shop/layout.tsx`, `app/track/page.tsx`. Confirmed via grep that zero of them had `alternates` set anywhere before this run.
- Added a self-referencing `alternates: { canonical: 'https://velorcommerce.store/<own-path>' }` to 11 of the 14: `/about`, `/apply`, `/apply/verified`, `/cookies`, `/founding`, `/help`, `/marketplace`, `/returns`, `/sell`, `/seller-agreement`, `/track`. Every value points a page at its own real URL -- nothing invented, no cross-page copying.
- Deliberately did NOT touch 3 of the 14, each for a specific, checked reason (all now recorded as backlog item 2/3 follow-ups, not silently dropped):
  - **Root `app/layout.tsx`** (the homepage's effective metadata, since `app/page.tsx` has no metadata export of its own): Next.js metadata merges per-field from root down, and any route segment that does NOT define `alternates` itself inherits the nearest ancestor's value. Checked (via the same repo-wide grep) that other public routes -- `/search`, `/legal/*`, `/contact`, `/terms` and others -- have NO metadata export at all, meaning they currently inherit the root's title/description already. Adding a homepage canonical to root right now would make every one of those un-audited routes silently claim its canonical URL is the homepage -- a real regression, not a fix. Left for a future run that first inventories and covers every public route.
  - **`app/shop/layout.tsx`**: same inheritance mechanism -- `/shop/[productId]` has no metadata of its own, so a canonical set on the shop layout would apply to every future product page too, wrongly telling search engines each product is really `/shop`. This is the same underlying gap as existing backlog item 2 (per-product metadata not built yet, catalogue near-empty) -- logged as one combined follow-up rather than shipping a canonical now that would need to be reverted later.
  - **`app/pulse/layout.tsx`**: private operations dashboard, already `robots: { index: false, follow: false }`. Not a public SEO surface; left alone.
- No copy, titles, or descriptions were changed anywhere -- purely additive, one new field per already-existing metadata object.

### 2026-07-12 18:27 UTC — Fixed backlog item 1: unique metadata on the 5 pages missing it

William asked directly to fix the 5-of-6 key pages sharing one generic title/description (backlog item 1 from the run above). Implemented it:

- Added `app/apply/layout.tsx`, `app/founding/layout.tsx`, `app/sell/layout.tsx`, `app/shop/layout.tsx`, and `app/help/layout.tsx` -- each a small server component that exports Next.js `metadata` (title, description, OpenGraph, Twitter card) and renders `{children}`. This is the standard App Router pattern for giving a `'use client'` page.tsx its own metadata without touching the existing client component at all: `app/apply/page.tsx`, `app/founding/page.tsx`, `app/sell/page.tsx`, `app/shop/page.tsx`, and `app/help/page.tsx` are all still `'use client'` and completely unedited.
- Every word of copy was drawn from the real, live copy already on each page (re-read each page's own `<h1>`/hero text and, for `/apply` and `/sell`, the confirmed `APPLICATION_SLA_HOURS = 24` constant in `lib/sellerApplicationReview.ts`, which already matches the literal "24 hours" text on both live pages) -- nothing invented, per LAW #1 and the copy-honesty comment at the top of `app/sell/page.tsx`.
- `/apply` and `/founding` (the seller-recruitment pages) and `/sell` were done first per the backlog's own stated priority; `/shop` and `/help` done in the same pass since the fix is identical and equally low-risk.
- `app/shop/layout.tsx` also covers `/shop/[productId]` (which had no metadata of its own) as a side benefit -- logged as a new, separate backlog item (per-product dynamic metadata once the catalogue has real listings), not attempted this run.

**Verification before pushing:** ran `npm install` and `npx tsc --noEmit` against the full repo (no `DATABASE_URL` available in this sandbox, and `prisma generate` failed with a 403 fetching engine binaries from `binaries.prisma.sh` -- a pre-existing sandbox network restriction, not caused by this change, so a full `next build` including the Prisma steps could not be run here). The `tsc` run surfaced ~150 pre-existing type errors across unrelated files (`lib/discount.ts`, `lib/fx.ts`, `lib/seller-ranking.ts`, etc.) but **zero errors touching any of the 5 new `layout.tsx` files** -- confirmed by grepping the output for their paths. The pattern used is identical to the one already live and building successfully in `app/layout.tsx` and `app/about/page.tsx`. This is a reasonable but not absolute confidence check; a full `next build` was not possible in this sandbox.

**Push:** committed and pushed directly via `git` over HTTPS with the PAT (same working method as the prior run). Confirmed on `origin/main` as commit `37b0211` ("SEO: add page-level metadata to /apply, /founding, /sell, /shop, /help") -- `git push` returned `b4a4d44..37b0211  main -> main` with no errors, and a follow-up `git fetch` + `git rev-list --count HEAD..origin/main` returned `0`, confirming local and remote `main` match exactly.

**Vercel deployment:** could not be checked directly from this sandbox (no Vercel login, no Chrome MCP/device bridge available).
